lines.st: To reclaim her kingdom from the foes, the red dragon of greed sought the aid of a shadowy force, the dark mist that haunted in the forest.

The red dragon breathed the fire of death, while the dark mist herded their foes into the blazing flames. Thus, the battle was won and the kingdom was hers. The thorn-choked fields began to stir with life once again.
Yet the deadly fire still crept across the land. The fields withered; the living faltered. The newborn perished before they could bloom, while the passing lingered, bound to this land.

The red dragon would not abide the death going unrestrained. Her wish was firm: life to life, death to death. Resolute in her purpose, she sought to cease the spreading fire. As Samhain drew near, she gathered those touched by the tainted flames into her lair.

lines.x: ...And that is the story of the red dragon.
But what comes after? What about the people tainted by the flames of death?
There is no 'after'. That's all I've written.
That simply will not do.
Everything has an ending, and those endings don't much care if you think they'll do, miss.
And yet it is for an altogether different reason that you hide yourself in this dilapidated pub after publishing your work. How ridiculous.
A lot of things are ridiculous.
Do you simply enjoy keeping people in suspense? Is your spirit soothed by tales of readers desperate to know the ending?
Some overly enthusiastic Messengers did manage to find me to deliver some fan mail.
You must have been rather pleased.
Pleased? I didn't even read them. Just tossed them into that fireplace.
How impertinent.
Moreover, not a single story in your collection doesn't cut off unceremoniously. Truly impertinent.
It's simple, really. I can't write an ending to save my life.
You'd best head home now, miss. You're clearly well-educated, and a ramshackle pub like this doesn't suit you. Don't fret yourself over those half-finished stories.
Why the hurry to show me the door? Perhaps I can help you.
The author takes measure of the woman once more. Even with a hood over her head, he can see how pale her face is. She must be very sickly, he thinks.
The moment she arrived in this abandoned village, she went straight for the pub. She had clearly done her research, and seems unlikely to give up anytime soon.
After ruminating, he picks up a sticky mug left among the cards and chips strewn across the table and downs a mouthful of rancid beer.
Alright then. So what kind of ending were you thinking?
Why should I be the one to come up with it?
You're the writer, not me. I simply wished to offer my own story, to spark your imagination.
Your own story?
Wait, are you another survivor? Did you also narrowly escape death in that city last autumn?
Only those who've experienced the truth for themselves would recognize what your stories are based on.
Well, go ahead then. I'm all ears.
Very well. Deep in the ruins of the former royal capital of Tara, long since destroyed by Catastrophe...
Deep in the ruins of the former royal capital of Tara, long since destroyed by Catastrophe, on one uneventful yet peaceful day, the flame burning in a metal stove was snuffed out.
This was no urgent matter, but the red dragon came all the same.
Does it resist being lit?
Or is it that not even an ominous outsider—newly come after centuries of silence—can relight old longings and resentments, near worn away by time?
Or... perhaps I simply need to be less reserved.
A purple flame dances around the stove.
Under its light, the red dragon's face looks even paler than before.
Are you awake?
Or must I remind you who you are?
The pathetic red dragon who cast aside his crown and wandered the wilderness for twenty years, only to be murdered by his closest kin on his return. Labhréathach the Exiled King, correct?
It truly is a pity, seeing someone still in shock at his own bloody death, six hundred years after the fact.
Six hundred years?
Give or take. The true date of your death has long been forgotten.
I'm shocked, yes, but not at my death. More at the fact that, after six hundred years, the Dracos have once again produced an aberration spewing the flames of death.
They even let you grow to adulthood without strangling you in the crib.
Did the Victorians bid you rouse me from death's slumber? Or did you hide that ridiculous crown from them beneath your bed?
Victoria no longer yokes the Red Dragon. The Tara of today heeds no foreign master.
Hah, not bad.
Tell me then, is it popular to wake the dead to deliver good news these days? But sorry, I've nothing to reward you with. Now if that's all, you'd best be on your way.
With my flames burning within you, you won't be ending this after a few trifling pleasantries, Labhréathach.
Ending? My ending came long, long ago. You're the one with unfinished business, yeah?

Look, I get it. They're the flames of death, after all. They'll get you into all kinds of trouble. Life and death getting jumbled up, herds of undying beasts, newborn cubs having their lives snuffed out before you know it, the works.
So, how big a mess are we talking here? A field? A city? A countryside?
I set all of Tara alight to free her from Victoria's heel.
You WHAT?!
Let her be razed to the ground. Let Tara become a nation of the dead. Either way, the flames of death will seek out new kindling. Or so I thought, at first.
But allowing so many lives to fade away simply because I am out of ideas, well, that would be awfully imprudent of me.
And so I came to you. Tell me, what should I do to cleanse these deathly flames from the fields of Tara?
...If only that were empty bluster.
Go ahead, use your imagination.
I've heard enough. What I'm about to say isn't for you, but for all the people still willing to call themselves Tarans.
Those tainted by your flame and marching towards death have been renounced by life itself. It will never return to them.
This goes without saying for the corpses marching behind you, obviously, but the same holds for those who still live yet walk like the dead. There's no medicine that can save them.
Gather them together, so that their fires do not taint others.
Appeasement. Is that all you can suggest?
Hah... Of course not.
Next, find another red dragon. Assuming you haven't already slaughtered your kin, of course.
Find a real red dragon, one whose flames warm others' lives. Let things return to as they once were. Ashes to ashes, dust to dust, life to life, death to death.
Is that all you know?
It is, ignorant young dragon.
Then it seems you don't know much more than I do.
The red dragon does not even bother to sneer at him. She simply turns and leaves.
She passes by a ruined home, faint yellow candlelight seeping through the cracks.
This is the wilderness where the dead roam. No one should be living here. And so, she knocks on the door.
Who're you?
Who I am is unimportant. What's important is, why are you living out here?
I came because there aren't any Victorians here to knock at my door and rob the food I dug up myself, along with my savings...
Five years ago, on a night like this, a wicked caster came by, killing the Victorians as they closed in, step by step.
I offered him beastmeat, grains, and freshly-brewed wine, but he told me he didn't need any of it. What he needed was fire, a fire fueled by those who would happily burn themselves away.
And so my only son, Keith, left home and followed him.
What a noble soul.
Then, three years ago, someone knocked on my door in the middle of the night again. It was Keith.
My Keith. He no longer spoke, or wept, or even breathed. He just stood there, his body covered in purple flames.
How tragic.
It gets worse. I buried him, but he refused to rot away in the earth. Instead, he dug himself out, and now his corpse is wandering endlessly the wilds.
Which is why I'm still living here.
Are you one of that caster's friends? Are you here to lay Keith to rest?
I am.
Ah, here he comes.
A walking corpse, wreathed in purple flame for all to see, shambles towards the two.
The red dragon presses the point of her spear against the corpse's chest. The fire rages so hot that it sears away its flesh in an instant, leaving nothing more than ash.
But an idle thought crosses the old man's mind: the fire did not seem to burn out, but instead went up along the spear to return to the red dragon's body.
And what of you? You're also tainted by the purple flames of death, are you not?
The old man looks himself over, but sees no flames licking his body like they did with his only son. But to the dragon, the flame consuming him was even brighter than that of the dead.
...If you mean sorrow, anger and hatred, then you're right, witch.
The Victorians taxed me in coin, while you and your ilk taxed me in blood. Together, you've taken all I have.
I've been empty inside for a while now. The only thing keeping me standing are those so-called flames of yours.
Then what if I were to take them back? What would you say to that?
I'd say 'you're late.'
The old man closes his eyes, facing the point of the red dragon's spear.
He can feel the fires of anger and sorrow propping him up burble as they flow into her hand. As they leave his body, his muddled consciousness leaves with them.
He can sense that death is near, but considering how stagnant his life has become, he no longer fears it.
But the fires suddenly stop flowing.
Why? Why won't you kill me?
Because you no longer need to be killed.
A spirit once told me that life will never again grace those tainted by the flames of death. Now I see that he was right.
'Now I see that he was right,' the red dragon answered coldly.
How is that for an ending? I took what I heard from the old man and added a bit of spice. It shouldn't be too jarring an addition to your story, I would think.
Throwing in the ghost of the legendary Exiled King, are we?
It'll do, but do you really need the ending to my story at this point?
What you've seen clearly differs from what I saw. I must see it through your eyes, hear it through your ears.
You're not one of those bored toffs who's into 'piecing together the truth', are you?
I have never once considered the truth boring.
Hah. Well, seeing as we both managed to make it out of that hellhole alive, I can pass you all the drafts I abandoned in the process of observing, thinking and writing.
But as for how those stories actually end, I couldn't say.
Since you were talking about the Red Dragon... how about I start there, too?
The writer pours another half-mug of beer directly into his belly, glances at the woman with a hint of provocation, then begins to tell his tale.
If you have ever made the journey to the Red Dragon's Lair we call Na Saoirsí, no matter how briefly, you will never forget the Dark Woods along the way.
They are dismal and damp, filled with a gloomy mist and fae who love playing tricks on men. A labyrinth of darkest black.
It is said that, of all the people who have entered, not a one has ever left without the forest's permission.
Even if it does open its boughs to allow you passage, the mischievous fae will still lead you astray, jeering at your clumsy movements all the while. But once they finally leave you be...
It will become as beautiful as a painting, a painting that should not exist in this realm.
The red dragon, along with the people who have followed her all this way, step into the lair without the slightest hesitation, leaving that beautiful sight behind them.
Na Saoirsí, the heart of Tara, 'the Red Dragon's Lair'...

lines.st: Hmm? 'Which red dragon,' you ask?
Guess you really do enjoy piecing together the truth. You must be thinking I think you don't know a damn thing, so I'm trying to mislead you?
Well, since you're already so well-informed, no need for me to beat around the bush.

lines.x: Na Saoirsí, the heart of Tara, 'the Red Dragon's Lair'... We're finally here.
Thank heavens! The whole trip from the Scáthanna Fields has felt like one long, long dream... Will my family really be safe here?
This land is more than just idyllic pastures and fields.
Tara's capital was never directly involved in Londinium's war, but there's been a never-ending spate of infiltrations and noble rebellions going on in the background around Na Saoirsí.
Especially after the Red Dragon marched her troops towards Londinium... It wouldn't be incorrect to say a minor war took place here.
Sorry, Reed. I was just happy as can be, didn't mean anything else by it!
No... a homecoming should be a happy affair.
Know any songs about the city or the Dark Woods, Moran?
Only the one, and it doesn't really fit the mood...
Oh, just sing it!
I stepped into the dark woods one morn, calling to the mists of hoar♪
I trod upon the road, heading out to war♪
No pipes a-singing, nor battle drums a-roar♪
Only the bells behind me, through dew and fog their rings do soar♪
This whole way back, I've been trying to come up with a new verse to add, but I've been lacking inspiration.
You seem even more anxious than before, Reed. Was the tune really that much of a downer?
No, it's nothing to do with the song. It's a good song.
I just...
Loughshinny does not finish her sentence.
Behind her, the refugees step out of the forest, one after the other.
Compared to the display of leaping colors they just emerged from, Na Saoirsí seems stiff and dour. However, aside from Loughshinny, nobody minds.
All they know is they have finally ended their long sojourn. They have reached journey's end.
They have returned home.
So, what next?
Someone will be coming to help us. Once they're here—
Loughshinny spots a figure slowly walking over from the city.
It must be the man here to greet them, but the last time they met, he had wanted to kill her.
She subconsciously puts her arms out to shield Moran at her side.
...Why are you here?
I have come to welcome you.
What?
I'll be escorting you into Na Saoirsí to meet with Her Highness Eblana.
Don't worry, you'll never have to see me again. Not unless absolutely necessary.
Indeed. The issues we could resolve without meeting, she and I already resolved on the Gastrell.
Then please, follow me. As for your entourage... Due to security concerns, I will make other arrangements for them.
No. They are Tarans, too. They must enter with me. I promised them.
While I would love to acquiesce, truth be told, the majority of our citizens are unaware that there is a second red dragon. Namely, you.
I can guarantee that they will keep it secret.
This is where you and Her Highness disagree.
Then do you mean to say that my sister will 'ensure' they do?
This is precisely why I must bring them into Na Saoirsí.
Err... I may have an idea that would satisfy both parties, if you'd care to hear it?
I brought you here, Wakewoman, not simply as an overseer for settling citizens, but also for your discretion in sensitive matters.
I hope this idea of yours isn't mere wishful thinking. If so, know that even you will no longer be welcome in Na Saoirsí.
Of course, of course.
As you so eloquently put it, Na Saoirsí is indeed unable to so readily open its gates to our errant countrymen.
But I'm headed to the village of the wakemen tomorrow to ask for some sacred instruments. Seeing how these people have nowhere else to go, I can have them stay at the village for now.
'Wakemen'? Isn't that some sort of codename plucked straight from the old legends?

My people have been relegated to song and story for many, many years, true. Those of us who live around the ruins rarely ever leave. I'm just an oddball.
Which is why I can promise you that...
well, no word of the red dragon will leave the village.
I really want to trust you, but...
Are you really one of them? The wakemen?
Moran?

Before my great-grandma passed, she asked for a traditional burial held by a wakeman. Our family searched all over as best we could, but never found one. All we could do was pass it off as the ramblings of a dying woman.
But she was a woman with her head on straight, not the type to mix things up with legends. If she said something's real, it's as real as can be. Besides, I've heard many stories about your kind.
I'm willing to trust you.
Then please don't betray that trust, Wakewoman.
Naturally.
...Alright.
What say you, Mr. Brigadier?
Go.
Nemos? Who are these people?
Sorry for making trouble for you even after I left.
They're Tarans with no place to call home. Na Saoirsí can't take them in at the moment, so I thought, maybe...
We made an exception many years ago by not forbidding you from leaving. We have even less reason to admit these strangers we know nothing about.
What if we're not here to be admitted to the wakemen, ma'am?
Moran?
Once, when I still dreamt of being a wandering bard, I collected the legends of the wakemen, sought out your people, and told your tales.
But in the end, my dreams of both bardhood and wakemen were shattered to pieces. Only now, when all I want is to go home, do I run into you.
If the legends are true, your people live ascetic lives. You only come into contact with outsiders to hold funerals, letting the souls of the departed find rest in fire.
...Correct.
We came here with... No, we're just folks from all across Tara seeking refuge. Not a one of us is qualified to join you. We're just looking for a place to stay for a day or two.
I promise you, we'll follow your laws and customs as best we can. We won't trouble you any more than we already have.
Winter's just around the corner, too. I'm not too sure if your people hold harvest festivals, but if you need any help, please, feel free to ask.
...Samhain. That is the true name of what you call the 'harvest festival'.
It would seem you really are different from the cityfolk.
There are some abandoned huts outside the ruins. You may clean them and stay there.
Thank you. Or rather, 'go raibh maith agat.'
Is there anything else, Nemos?
The Dublinners in the city want to hold a victory party to celebrate Tara's liberation.
I promised them I'd ask you for some of the sacred instruments. They'd show Taran unity, and represent the importance of tradition to the nation.
With Samhain almost upon us, we have none to spare. Have you forgotten? But even if we did, we would not give them away.
They're only requesting them because of how much they value the old ways...
Then why is it that not a single denizen of Na Saoirsí has come to us to request a funeral since the city became independent?
We can tell the difference between remembering a name once forgotten, and dressing yourself up in a name you've read in a history book.
But if you cannot return empty-handed, then take this whistle and go.
This is... just an ordinary whistle...
It is the highest honor we can give them.
The wakemen do not stand with the living, nor do we require their reverence. Until, of course, they are dead.
That is the tradition Dublinn should be honoring.
Extra! Extra! Read all about it!
The Victorians are retreating, not fighting!
Tara has gotten back all its territory!
Peace will be restored to all of Tara by Samhain!
Stop the cart.
I've been ordered to escort you directly to meet Her Highness, with no delays.
Before I meet her, I need to see it with my own eyes—my sister's city, post-war Tara, the survivors and civilians—have their new lives finally begun?
...If you insist.
But the people are not yet prepared for two red dragons—
Of course I know that.
Just—just take these!
These knives, axes, and bowls are all made of iron. They'll make for good materials.
I beg you, Culann, take them... even if you have to melt them down and make some eyes for our statue.

lines.st: Autumn is almost over, and mornings in Na Saoirsí are cold and damp.
A pair of scarred, weathered hands rub against the cold and dry metal goods—much like a soldier running their fingers over a medal of valor in wartime.
The people yell out his name, hoping the broken tools in their hands will be accepted, smelted, and used by the hero to forge the statue.
The hero... yes, the hero... Every story needs a hero like that.

lines.x: Theo! Theo! Come help carry this stuff into the workshop.
I mean now, Theo! I'll help you sharpen your knife tonight.
What a lazy brat. This is what you get for pampering him too much.
No, uh, I mean, doesn't matter if that boy's around, just take everything, please!
I can't take everything. We all need these tools to get by. And that goes double for your prosthetic leg.

I—
I'm standing up fine, aren't I?
That wooden leg Nemos made is only temporary. Once better craftsmen arrive in town, they can repair your iron one.
But you're making a statue to commemorate our victory in the war—one that'll be displayed in the main square during Samhain!
Frankly, it'd be an honor for my rusty old leg to be a part of that.
No means no.
Just take it, already. It's all old stuff we'd have thrown out while cleaning anyway—no one's got any use for them.
What's wrong with these bowls? They look perfectly fine to me.
We can use clay ones instead. As for the axes, we'll just share the few we've got left.
The war just ended, and the city's short on everything. If we don't help you, how will the statue ever be finished?
Still, I can't accept used prosthetics, no matter what.
You're a great man, my hero, but you can be so stubborn!
Loughshinny slowly walks into the crowd.
For some reason, she feels as though there's something separating her from them. As if she's looking at them from the other side of a bonfire.
Excuse me, what are you all doing?
A new face in town? Haven't seen many of those lately! Where are you from, Vouivre?
I've returned to Tara from a place far, far away.
See, Culann? If you'd just taken everything without a fuss, you wouldn't have embarrassed yourself in front of our countryman from afar.
My answer is still no. Not the prosthetic leg. The other stuff... fine.
Why are people donating their pots, pans, and prosthetics?
Because we all want to erect a statue in the square for Samhain, to celebrate Tara's rebirth.
It's to celebrate Samhain?
I've only ever come across the festival in Taran folklore. It's held after the autumn harvest and before winter, yes? And it's meant to connect this shore to the next.
You know your stuff, Vouivre!
We used to just celebrate the harvest every year. It was the Dublinners who told us about the festival's historical origins.

So we sweep our houses, prepare feasts for our guests, and most importantly, light bonfires! On the day when life and death intertwine, we welcome all visitors to the banquet... There's so much to do!
And since this is the first Samhain since Tara's rebirth, it should be even more grand.
Good thing, too. Otherwise, Culann would never accept our old pots even if he had to tear down his own house instead.
Speaking of, do you have any iron of your own you'd like to donate, so the hero can add it to the statue?
All I have on me is a bookmark. I think it's made of iron. Will that do?
You don't need to listen to her, Taran from afar.
Haha...
So what sort of statue are you making?
A red dragon, to celebrate Tara's rebirth.
A red dragon...
I'm telling you, Culann, if you don't take my prosthetic leg, I—I won't leave until you do!
Then I suppose I'll have to pick you up and carry you back myself.
Ugh! You and your gigantic muscles! By the time Samhain is over and the statue has been erected, we'll all be living the good life. We'll be able to make as many prosthetics as we want!
Just imagine: a massive dragon statue standing tall in the night. Even through winter, its flames will remain lit, just like in the legends. Why it'd be enough to drive off the man-eating mist...
And we wouldn't have to obey that blasted curfew anymore—
The curfew is an order approved by the Red Dragon, put in place to ensure our safety.
I know that. Of course I know that!
As they speak, more and more people begin to gather around them, until the alley is completely blocked. They each carry all manner of things meant to be melted down for the statue.
Culann patiently receives each new visitor as the objects at his feet begin to pile up. He's quite a large man, but even he can't carry all of them at once.
Allow me to help you, Culann.
Oh, no need for that. My apprentice, Theo—
Can you really count on Theo, though? We have no idea where that rascal ran off to! He talks a good game, but slacks off when no one's watching—typical Victorian!
And how old was he when his parents died? Does he even remember Victoria?
So what's your name, friend?
Reed.
Have you found a place to stay yet? You're welcome to stay at my place for the night—you wouldn't want to be wandering the streets during curfew, that's for sure.
I think I do. But... a curfew? You mean the city has a lights-out bell?
Not quite. Victoria used lights-out bells to suppress the Tarans. Na Saoirsí's curfew is to safeguard us.
The black mist lingers in the Dark Woods, shielding all of Na Saoirsí from outsiders, but at times, it also drifts into the city streets.
The curfew is meant to prevent anyone from encountering the mist. It permits no trespass—anyone who enters without permission will never return.
You mean the mist really eats people?
...Listen to yourself. You come all this way, and this is what you ask about? Look, things will be better soon, just you wait.
Now come with me. I'll show you around Na Saoirsí.
What about the man building the statue? Culann, was it? What is he going to do? I know a bit of Arts. Perhaps I could help him.

Oh, don't worry about him! He's a genuine hero! He once dispatched an entire Victorian scout team in the Dark Woods. Unfortunately, he was a bit of a softy at the last moment. He's the only one willing to put up with Theo's nonsense.
All by himself? Really?
Well, in the woods. The forest has always been on the Tarans' side—Ugh! Stop changing the subject, Reed!
Just come with me and have some ale and stewed meat. It'll warm you right up!
You must know that you've come home at the perfect time.
Phew. Well, at least there are still potatoes in the store...
Damn! Look at the time! I'm late!
Who's the one charging through here like a wrecking ball—?
Theo?
Sorry, sorry. I'm just in a hurry to get back. I didn't mean to bump into you, sir. Here. Let me wipe that off for you.
...You dare to disrespect me, too?
What? No, no. Maybe I misspoke. I was just wondering if you needed help—
Stay away from me!
The man raises his cane and strikes Theo hard on the chest, knocking him away.
The morning mist has long since gathered in the uneven crevices of the stone pavement, forming murky puddles. Caught off guard, Theo stumbles backward and falls straight into one.
You Victorian scoundrel! Who do you think you are? How dare you offer me charity?!
Urgh...!
Put that cane away! What's going on here?
Are you okay, Theo?
I'm fine, Brigid, I'm fine. I crashed into him first.
A rootless nomad and a Victorian mongrel—couldn't ask for a more perfect pair of lowlives.
You're not exactly making your ancestors proud, Brigid.
What's that supposed to mean?
Hmph. Forget the Victorian mongrel. What about you?
You're descended from the Exiled King, from the very clan that's supposed to warn the Tarans of impending Catastrophes—yet here you are, mingling with the likes of him.

Even during the war, you never cared whether your own people won or lost, you'd just coldly toss those battle reports into the citadel, then go door to door delivering those meaningless personal letters...
Are you lot even fit to call yourselves Tarans?
So, this is the only place you have to go? Why not just go back to Culann?
I... I need to wash my clothes first. Should be quick. You don't need to worry about me, Brigid.
Of course I'm worried. I came here just to see you, after all.
Hmph. How is a place wrecked by war and left to rot any better than Culann's workshop? At least there you'd have the forge keeping you warm, and hot meals.
No, it's just that... I don't want to cause him any more trouble. Please—don't tell Culann about what happened today. Okay? I'll be on my way as soon as my clothes dry.
I'm not here for him, Theo. I'm here for you, to take you away.
Take me way? Did he say something to you?
Just what sort of relationship do you have with him, anyway?
If I hadn't shown up today, were you going to take the beating without fighting back? My instincts are never wrong: if you stay in the city, they'll just keep finding new ways to push you around.
Today, they shoved you into a puddle and beat you with a cane. Tomorrow, who knows?
Anyhow, just come with me. I can take you to the plains, or anywhere else you fancy. Up to you.
You and me, we don't belong here. And if you've got nowhere else to go, you can always head to Nemos's old place and stay with the wakemen for a while.
I do want to see them, after all the stories Nemos has told me...
But I can't leave, not unless Culann asks me to.
Nemos has no time to look after him, and the dragon statue isn't finished yet. What would he do if I just up and left?
But still, thanks Brigid.
If you say so. Then I'll take you back to the city before I leave.
No, don't bother. It's cold out, so my clothes won't be dry for a while. I'll go back then.
And if all else fails, I can always take shelter in that abandoned home. Someone left a quilt and bedsheets there, and I've stayed there before. It'll be fine.
...If you really don't want to come with, well, I suppose it's safer here than risking a trip to another city.
So not even Culann looks after you all the time, huh?
He has more important things to do. I should be making things easier on him, not giving him something else to worry about.
Well, goodbye then. Take care of yourself, you hear?
We're here!
Like I said, you've come at the perfect time. Today we have fresh ale, stew, and oatmeal... if only we had a bonfire.
Shouldn't you save that for Samhain?
According to the old customs, we can actually light it a few days before Samhain.
Then again, the war just ended, so we really don't have much in the way of firewood. We can't just start chopping down trees, either, because of the curfew.
At any rate, it's still light out. The sun can be our bonfire today!
But that's enough chit-chat—take a seat. I'll have them make room for you.
Loughshinny steps forward. In the damp, chilly air, each dish in the lavish spread on the table has the same familiar scent.
Warm, rustic, and simple, yet not what it seems.
She looks around, concealing her surprise, only to find that no one else finds any of this unusual.
...Is this stewed meat?
Yeah. This here is the meat, these are grilled fins, and your cup is full of ale. Oh, right, and we also have oatmeal. I'll go grab you a bowl.
But...
All the dishes appear to be made of the same thing.
They're all made of potatoes, right?
Their statue is bowls. Their bonfire is the sun. Their feast is... potatoes.
As the friendly resident goes to fetch a bowl of 'oatmeal', Loughshinny stands up and walks toward the cart-and-burdenbeast that has been waiting for her the whole time.
Her Highness will return very soon. Please wait here.
And this place is...?
Na Saoirsí citadel. The highest point in the city is just up the steps beside you. This is also where Her Highness lingers most, when she's in the city.
I'm glad to see you aren't filling the halls with candlelight, musicians, and lavish feasts.
I've already told you: the primary reason the city is short on supplies is because trade between Victoria and Tara has not yet fully resumed. We're working as hard as we can to resolve the issue.
The residents are very understanding of the situation. They hold nothing but goodwill and genuine love for Tara.
Your Highness Eblana!
Leave us.
At once!
Loughshinny.
Just what is going on in this city, Sister?
Everyone's celebrating Samhain, except there's no harvest, no bonfire, and the feast is nothing but potatoes!
If you came here to make me repeat what the Brigadier has already told you, well, I suppose I can play along. I wouldn't want to spoil our long overdue reunion.
I'm more worried about your spear. Where is it now?
My spear?
If you're talking about the spear you gave me, it's being stored in a warehouse on Rhodes Island. I no longer need it.
How can someone with no weapon claim any sort of power? Or forget power—even survival would be a struggle.
I said 'the spear you gave me.'
Also, you won't kill me. At least, not yet.
As a puppet that has only just regained its soul, you wouldn't want to break me before you've had a chance to play with your new toy.
Your words are... quite hurtful.
Anyone can be made a puppet, Loughshinny, anyone except you. You are my dance partner, yet you'd rather stand in my shadow than join me on the floor.
Until about a year ago. You finally got bored of me, didn't you?
...I don't recall what you're referring to.
As for power, I'm not here for power. That power rightly belongs to you.
Oh?
You've been setting fires all across Tara. Those fires have melted the chains that once bound the Tarans. That much is true.
Then why have you returned?
To bear witness.
If you're truly willing to use your power to give the Tarans a new beginning, then I will do everything I can to support you.
Say, for example, that you're too busy to deal with the supply shortages in the city right now. If so, I would be willing to help with that.
Whether I step forward as a second red dragon, or hide my identity as before, it makes no difference to me.
As noble as ever, my dear sister.
But I can't say whether...
...you would truly be satisfied with that.
The sister I knew wasn't someone who sought power simply to live a life of indulgence. She wanted more than that.
But so far, every step you've taken has been paved with bones and blood.
I can only pray that's because you've had no other choice.
I sincerely hope I will never have reason to collect my spear and come searching for you.
Not bad. Not bad at all.
It seems your life of wandering has taught you far more than you ever learned while acting as my shadow.
Let's not beat around the bush. I've made my intentions clear, and you've invited me back. So what is it you want from me?
You'll find out, soon enough.
Until you do, though, why not come with me? I'll show you around the Red Dragon's Lair.
Legend has it that the architect deliberately shaped the plate with uneven elevations to mirror the old Taran capital.

Legend has it that Tara's stonemasons showcased their finest craftsmanship in every building they made—save for one. They refused to add even a single ornament to the citadel, knowing it would be inhabited by a puppet king.
Loughshinny has heard many stories like this, but no story can tell you whether it is itself a true flame, or merely its shadow cast on the wall.
And so, she follows her sister up the steps and looks down at the city with her own eyes.
She sees the empty pedestal in the square below. It seems to be waiting for something massive to be placed upon it.
She sees the winding, undulating cobblestone roads stretching from the square, and the humble stone houses, so simple they inspire a sense of affection.
But after she focuses her gaze and takes a harder look, she finally sees the thing she had sensed before, the thing that had separated her from the other residents.
It is something that definitely does not belong in the city.
Flames?!
Flames.
At a closer look, there are tiny sparks of fire scattered across the city—all the residents, whether talking, rushing to and fro, yelling, or resting, are surrounded by flickering flames.
Purple flames.
What did you do to these people? You've made them into walking corpses, haven't you?!
You just got back from town. You met them yourself. You should know better than I do.
The flames will spread.
Your flames of death should not be spread to the living.
It is an unfortunate reality, but wherever man resides, so too does death. Whether on the battlefield or in a bustling city. The flames merely hasten the inevitable.
But I must admit, it doesn't sit right with me either.

The dead wander the wilderness, driven by the desires they held in life. While those tainted by the flames of death can never truly start anew... Life and death cannot remain entangled like this, each defiling the other.
But... haven't you been their chief defiler?
No. Death should be mine to command. I cannot have it expanding beyond the boundaries I set.
Well then, my dear sister, what would you like Tara to become? I want your opinion.
Should the living endure and the dead rest in peace? Or should all of Tara become a kingdom of death?
Why ask a question you already know the answer to?
Then it is clear what you must do.
Kill me, Loughshinny.
What?!
While you've been trudging through the crowds, I've been busy preparing.
The moment the tip of your spear pierces my heart, heralding death's embrace, everything tainted by my deathly flames shall leave this realm along with me.
Then, you will become the first and only true leader Tara has seen in decades.
...I will not be your shadow anymore, and we red dragons will not be the monsters they claim us to be—slaughtering our own kin in madness.
Sometimes—often, even—kinslaying can be a virtuous act.
What about those touched by your fire—can they really start their lives anew, as you promised?
I have promised nothing about life, Loughshinny, not even for those untouched by my flames.
As for the cityfolk, they have already been entangled by the violet flames. They will gradually become walking corpses, and eventually leave here with me. This much is certain.
They are living, breathing people! They fought under your command and won your war for you—they have the right to start anew!
An unfortunate fate, to be sure, but their fate nonetheless.
No, Eblana. It shall not be their fate.
What will you do, then?
Loughshinny has more to say, but Eblana has already turned around and started walking down the long corridor. Her footsteps are so soft that Loughshinny did not even realize she had left.
After her sister's figure disappears completely into the darkness, Loughshinny turns her gaze back to the city.
How she wishes it were all just one of Eblana's tricks... but the flames are real.
She clenches her fists, pulls up her hood, and turns to descend the steps.
(*whispers*) Sorry.
Ma'am! Hold on, please!
Oh, Reed! It's you! You left so suddenly—I was worried our hospitality had been lacking in some way.
No, I truly appreciated your hospitality. I had just suddenly remembered some business I had to take care of. I still have a few questions, though. Would you mind answering them for me?
Ask away.
Do you realize that you're engulfed in flames?
Flames? Oh, you mean my blessing? Wow, you can see other people's blessings?

I didn't notice until someone pointed it out to me—
Wait, you're saying you know about the flames, but can't see them? How can that be?
The friendly resident puts her hand on her waist.
The ancient Tarans carried small flames at their waists, stabilized through Originium Arts, to dispel the dark mist in the marsh.
After Victoria seized full control of Tara, this custom—along with all similar practices—was strictly banned.
But times have changed. No longer bound by Victoria's rules, the Tarans, or at least the people of Na Saoirsí, have chosen to wear small Originium lamps at their waists.
I'm sure you know fuel is scarce, and with the curfew in place, we can't afford to keep the lamps on—nor do we need to. But... if we carry these small lamps...
That's her fire... That's the color of her fire in the lamp.
Now, whenever people turn on their lamps, this is the color they'll see. Even if they replace the filaments with different ones, the color remains unchanged.
And you're not the least bit worried about this?
Well, as you can see, things are on the up and up here in Na Saoirsí. And what explanation can there be but the Red Dragon's blessing?
But—
But?
But if a reckless returnee tried to warn her that those flames were anything but fortuitous, she would never believe it.
Loughshinny hopes that perhaps a more direct approach would work.
But I wanted to experience the dragon's blessing for myself. May I hold your hand?
But you've already entered the city. Everyone who does will receive the blessing, sooner or later.
Please? It's just a small favor.
Well, I suppose it wasn't easy for you to make it all the way back to Tara.
Oh, right, what do you do for a living, anyway?
I... I know some basic medicine, along with some healing Arts. Nothing to brag about.
So you're a doctor, then. We're in need of doctors, actually.
Come here. Give me your hand—
She tightly clasps the woman's calloused hand, letting the fire spread from her own wrist to Loughshinny's.
Thank you...
She shuts her eyes.
Close your eyes and remember those whose pain you once soothed, those no longer forced to linger in this realm—
Go, and warm this cold fire.
Ugh...
The purple flames gradually fade. Beneath them, a pale face emerges, swaying gently, with a friendly smile.
So she tries to erase more of the purple flames, but fails to notice when the woman's expression suddenly changes.
My God, I—
My hand! What did you do to my hand?! It's completely numb!
What?!

I can't feel my blessing anymore, either! Just what are you doing to me?!
Let go!
Reed!
Wakewoman?
Reed, please let go of this poor lady.
I—
(Shakes head)
Sorry, ma'am.
She means no harm—I stake my honor on it.
But she said she's a doctor—how could a doctor possibly...?
Arts-based medicine can sometimes have side effects. Look at your hand, it's perfectly fine now, isn't it?
But I still can't feel anything—
You'll be fine.
The wayward wakewoman raises a hand, gently pressing her warm palm to the frightened woman's forehead. It is just a normal, casual gesture, yet the woman gradually calms down.
Okay, okay. I believe you. Right, I still have my hand, as well as my blessing.
It's almost nightfall. You should head back home.
And Reed, you have another banquet to attend, don't you?
A banquet?
...Right, the banquet.
So please, allow me to lead the way.
Wakewoman, since you've returned, does that mean Moran and the others have already settled in?
That's right. They'll be staying just outside the ruins. They're probably still clearing out the empty houses right about now.
Thank you.
Don't mention it. By the way, call me Nemos. I hardly do any work as a wakewoman these days.
Alright, Nemos. You mentioned a banquet just now. Who's hosting it?
In a few days, Dublinn will be hosting a celebratory banquet, and all the high-ranking nobles will be in attendance, including Her Highness Eblana. We've been very busy with all the preparations.
A celebratory banquet? While the residents are making do with 'stewed meat' and 'grilled fins' made from only potatoes?

The potato thing started out due to a food shortage, but now, they're just...
used to it.
But Dublinn is aware of the situation in the city. They're calling it a 'banquet', but I didn't see any eating or drinking on the itinerary.
I'm not trying to cover for anyone, Miss Loughshinny. I truly believe that Dublinn wouldn't turn its back on the people of Tara like that.
Now if you'll excuse me, I must be going.
She certainly left in a hurry.
So if directly removing the violet flames would only cause a panic, what if I were to do it more subtly...?
To lunder whatever you want, then?
Who's there?
The noble red dragon turns around and sees a figure engulfed in blazing purple flames.

No, if the other residents have been merely 'kissed' by the flames, then the person standing before her seems to have been forged from the purple fire itself. The flames don't look harsh, yet they distort the very light around them.
Unfortunately, what you want sounds incredibly boring.
After all, Tara's flames have already been dyed that color. That's just a fact.
You know about the flames.
I happened to find out about them, yes.
Tell me everything you know. It's extremely important.
Why should I?
I need to stop people from being engulfed by the purple flames.
So you can replace them with your own flames?
As long as the people of the city are safe, the color doesn't matter.
What grandiose nonsense. It's almost nostalgic. Your excuse is so clumsy that it's laughable, red dragon.
What is your relationship to my sister, sir?
Loughshinny takes a step forward, while the figure moves backwards, preserving the distance between them.
Relationship? You, your sister, and I are all cut from the same cloth. We all crave power, conflict, and bloodshed.
We're all itching for what comes next, eager to set the land ablaze.
But this time, I'll stay out of it. Until the Lair is consumed by flames, I'll be cheering on both sides.
Goodbye—
Wait!
The red dragon tries to weave the flames of life into a cage, but before it can take shape, the strange figure scatters in the wind.
Her fire only briefly illuminated his long tail...
Then returned to nothingness.
Sir.
It's you. What's wrong? Why have you come to see me now?
Don't tell me there's another problem with your grain shop that I have to deal with. I've never treated you as a subordinate—we're 'business partners', understand?
I understand.
Good, as long as you do. I have another shipment that needs to be escorted out of the city. Leave soon, and be back before curfew.
Sir, about that—
About what?
About the grain we sent out before... You said it wouldn't affect the city's food supply, but now we're all stuck eating only potatoes...
Do you know what it's like in other parts of Tara? They don't even have potatoes!
Na Saoirsí's agricultural output is already top-tier. We're taking urgent measures to help our countrymen. Understand?
Besides, how much did you earn from just those last two carts?
Look at it this way: the people of Na Saoirsí aren't starving. At least they've got potatoes to fill their bellies.
What's more, it's almost harvest time. There will be plenty of new grain then. I'm more worried that your tiny shop won't have the throughput to sell it all.
Still, I'm not one to force anyone to act against their conscience. If you really feel that bad about it, you can always just transfer your business license to someone else and close your shop.
But how will I eat then?
I told you, you're not my subordinate. How is that my problem?
Alright, alright, just hand over your business license by the end of the day—
No! Don't! Okay! I'll do it.
Hmph.
*Sigh*...
Why are you still standing there?
I have something else to discuss. My friend told me that lately, the dark mist in the forest...
Dark mist? If I needed you to remind me about that, I might as well quit right now.
There will be someone waiting for you once you're past the gates, just like always. Anything else is none of your concern. Now go.
Sir.
What is it? Did The Brigadier say something when he was on patrol this morning?
No sir, he didn't come around this morning. The Leader sent him on another assignment.
Phew.
Then what's got you in such a panic?
It's officially harvest season for the agricultural plate to the south.
Isn't that a good thing?
Yes, but, uh, all the skilled agricultural workers have left the city.
Tch, just follow the handbook—do exactly as it says, got it?
Yes sir!
By the way, what's the estimated yield for the entire plate? Report it to me, and I'll consolidate the numbers later.
Um, we're not exactly familiar with this sort of work either...
You're useless! Can't you at least tell when the crops are ready for harvest?
They're definitely ripe, or we wouldn't have harvested them in the first place...
Then isn't it just business as usual? Report a normal yield based on previous years! Now get going!
Hello, I'm Brigid the nomad.
What do you want?
I've delivered all my letters in Na Saoirsí. So I'd like to go to the highest point in the city and let the wind carry my message to my brothers and sisters. Then I'll be on my way.
Ah, now I remember—it's you. You've been here so many times, and we've never let you up there even once. Haven't you learned your lesson yet?
Even the Victorians let me up to the top of the citadel when they were in charge.
Do I look like a Victorian to you?
No. You're a Taran.
Then you should know that, since we're all Tarans, there's no need to indulge those outdated customs of yours anymore. All that nomad and wakeman nonsense—it's all stupid superstition.
Now get moving! If you don't, I might just have you arrested!
Fine, I'm going. No need to make a fuss...
Welp, I've done exactly nothing I set out to do, other than delivering my letters.
Oh well. At least I'm out of the city before curfew. Now to just pass through the Dark Woods and I'll be back on the open plains.
*Sigh*... I just hope the mist will let me through.
But it's never stopped the nomads before, so why am I worrying about it?
The nomad kicks a pile of fallen leaves by the roadside. The rustling sound echoes through the vast, empty forest ahead.
To her surprise, the sound doesn't fade away—it echoes back in a repeating loop.
Brigid suddenly crouches down, trying to cover the scattered leaves before her. Where the sound is coming from.
Sorry, shouldn't have kicked you.
The sound continues.
Uh—
It doesn't stop. Instead it grows louder and louder, echoing until it becomes an unbearable, deafening cacophony.
At the same time, the dark mist creeps up from her feet, covering her eyes and ears.
Until she realizes she is back at the forest entrance.
....This is the fifth time!
Not going to let me leave? Really? Forcing me back into the city, is that it?
Hmph. Well, I'm not going back.
I'll set up a tent outside the forest if I have to. I'll get my chance to leave, sooner or later.
Wow. There are so many people here...
Sorry. Please make way—
—Aah!
I've been to Millington Street already. It was picked clean ages ago! The shops are still open, but the shelves are all empty! Even the shopping bags are gone!
Kraman said they had stuff left here, so I ran five blocks only to find not even a single fowlbeast feather left!
Might as well go back, grab my crossbow, and sneak past the guards to hunt in the Dark Woods—at least I might get some real meat before I starve.
...What?
You've really gone crazy from hunger now—going into the forest is a death wish!
And you're not crazy? Stop talking like you know anything!
Who are you to say I don't know anything? The grain store on Lagran Street, Emma's place, you know about it?
Haven't had a chance to check it out yet. What of it?
I went there and had a look. There's no grain left, but from what Emma says, it's all because of those bloody Victorians!
The Victorian bastards have been blockading us since forever. This isn't new, so why act like it's a big shock?
Because their spies have already infiltrated Na Saoirsí!
Come again?
When Emma went to visit the ag plate, she saw the charred remains with her own eyes. Dublinn is still investigating!
Bloody hell! We should've struck back the minute Tara gained independence!
You're right about one thing though: you should go get your crossbow. Not to hunt, but to root out the traitors!
Of course, if I caught them, I wouldn't let them off that easy. A quick death is too good for them.
(*Whispers*) I guess the stores have been cut off from the supply of grain...
(*Whispers*) I boiled a few potatoes for Culann, but is it enough?
(*Whispers*) There are too many people here. I'll take another route.
(*Whispers*) Lots of them are armed too...
(*Whispers*) Are they planning to hunt down Victorian spies? But there's no grain left in the city... what does that have to do with the Victorians?
(*Whispers*) But I....
(*Whispers*) I...
I'll be fine. I'm Mr. Culann's apprentice—everyone knows that.
Don't be afraid, Theo. No one will hurt you.
Theo crouches down and squeezes through the crowd, making his way to the opposite corner of the street. He nimbly slips through a dark, narrow passage and emerges on the other side of a brick wall.
The noise of the crowd gradually fades from his ears.
....See? No one gave me any trouble.
Mr. Culann must still be busy with the dragon sculpture. By the time he notices what's going on around him, all the stores will be empty.
I need to help him.
Take these and give them to the neighbors. Tell them not to worry.
What about you? Or Nemos? Will she have anything to eat?
Instead of giving us grain, it'd be more useful to help everyone catch the spies. Otherwise, we'll never get to the root of the problem.

...I'll go. Please let everyone know.
But for now, just take them.
Nemos stands with The Leader, so it's only natural to endure a little hardship for everyone's sake.
...Alright then. Thank you.
Hey Culann, you must have more where that came from, right?
Besides, how'd you know we'd be running out of grains? Did Nemos tip you off in advance, or...?
What are you saying, Rory?!
If you don't want any, fine.
How do I know you or Nemos aren't spies? You've both been raising that Victorian boy together!
...Get out.
What, did I hit a nerve? Am I right? Are you really—
Culann! I heard you've been distributing grain! Impeccable timing! So where is it?
Everything that's left is right here.
Don't change the subject. You still haven't answered me.
I've said everything I had to say. If you feel these potatoes will dirty your hands, then give them to someone else.
Why you—
Do you want them or not, Rory? If not, then let me have them. There are plenty of hungry bellies to feed. Don't be ungrateful.
Now give them here—
Why should I?
You don't want them, right? So give them to me!
Fat chance!
Ugh—Aaahh!
The potatoes in the bag were very small. None had the usual oval shape.
My arm!
My God! He's bleeding! How could you turn a blade on a fellow Taran, Rory?!
I, I—
Drop the sword.
Don't make me use my hammer on you.
Y-You're the only one we can count on, Culann.
Something's been off about Rory lately, I'll follow him a bit, to check up on him. He's been out of sorts ever since his antique collection got burned in the war.
As for his blade, just take it. Don't let him have it back.
Culann... I... Ughh...
The wound isn't too deep. Come here, I'll stop the bleeding. As far as the grains, I'll have Nemos think of a solution when she gets back.

I know... ow ow ow... I know. You're giving away everything you have.
But you know, you're not as scary as you look—
Ow!
Don't talk. Save your energy.
I might have some cornmeal left at home. Let me check.
You can have it, then I'll walk you home.
Culann! Are you and Theo doing alright—
...Why is there blood in here?
Rory lost control and slashed someone.
Where's Theo?
He left a while ago for—
Dammit!

You—
*Sigh* Forget it. No one could have foreseen things getting so bad, so quickly.
How are things in the city?
Rumors are spreading everywhere that the Victorians destroyed the ag plate, and Na Saoirsí is about to boil over.
I know no one dared to go after Theo after you stood up for him, so you felt safe letting him run around. But this time... it's different.
I have to go find him.
Do you have time for that? Aren't you making that statue?

It only needs to be finished before Samhain. There's still a little time left...
Dammit! Dammit!
I'll worry about Theo. You just focus on your work.
Okay.
Have you seen Theo?
Theo? Where has that boy run off to... Slacking off again, no doubt. Culann spoils him too much. You really should talk to him about that.
Theo, Theo!
What trouble has that boy gotten himself into this time?
Have you seen Theo?
Are you alright?
...Me? Yeah, I'm fine. Just... hungry. So what is it? Did you say something?
Just the person I was looking for! Has that boy Theo been up to anything suspicious lately?
...No. Just keeping himself busy, as usual.
Hmph, well he'd better.
Theo!
What are you doing here, Wakewoman?
Wait, you're—oh, 'The Singer'. What brings you here?
I heard rumors of chaos in the city, so I thought there might be something I could do to help. Alas, there is not. All I've heard is that the supply shortage was caused by Victorian sabotage.
I've only ever run into the Victorian army by accident, and you and Culann were the ones to save me at the time.
Also, the information you've provided us on the wakemen has been truly fascinating—
Ah, sorry sir, but I really don't have time to be reminiscing. But I could use your assistance. Culann's apprentice, Theo, has gone missing. Can you help me look for him?
I am... unable to help with that.
The cityfolk are angry enough at the Victorians to flay one alive. Why bother running around looking for a Victorian child?
Forget it then. I'll go look myself.
Remember that the celebration will start in just over an hour. Don't you need to deliver the sacred instruments symbolizing the wakemen's blessings to the venue?
Gah!
*Sigh* Sorry, I really must be going now.
What do I do now...?
Nemos? Is that you?
I've finally found you. What happened in the city?
I've checked every inhabited street, and found no sign of Theo anywhere. All I saw were the flames on the townsfolk growing even stronger, as if a sudden gale had swept through.
What's more, everyone just seems used to it at this point. Getting rid of the flames would only terrify them. They'd only have numbness in their body.
Does this mean it's too late for the people of Na Saoirsí?
...No. I can't think that way. My first priority is to find Theo. I should go find that nomad named Brigid, the one Nemos mentioned.
But... would anyone really be willing to pitch a tent outside the city, just because they can't get into the Dark Woods?
'Walk out along the right side of the guard outpost, then wade through the creek'...
Ah. I think this is it.
A Perro with blonde hair. Tall, yet agile.
This must be the Brigid Nemos mentioned—
You know me?
No, I don't believe we've met before. Nemos asked me to come find you. My name is Reed.
You...
Brigid leans in slightly and twitches her nose twice but does not otherwise react. Loughshinny takes this to be the way the nomads greet each other.
You're not the red dragon from the city.
What? I'm not a red dragon at all, I'm just—
You're not the one from the city—you're a different one. A second dragon returning to the city... just like the old stories.
And you can tell with a single sniff?
Sure can. Your flames smell different.
But that's none of my business. What can I do for you, Reed?
Theo's gone missing. Nemos told me you might have an idea where he is. I've heard you often chat with him when you're in town.
He's missing?!
He went out this morning and never came home, and the city's in chaos today. We searched all over Na Saoirsí, both Nemos and I, but there was no sign of him anywhere.
But I saw him just before I left the city! He was doing fine—
No, I can't say he was 'fine'...
Anyhow, I went to the citadel after that, then left town. Haven't seen him since.
Where the heck could he be?
Theo! Theo!
Should've knocked him out and carried him back with me when I had the chance.
Say something, Theo! This time you're coming with me, you hear?!
Um, where are we, Brigid?

This plate was dragged out during the war, supposedly to appease some noble or other. But when they brought it back, it was left like this, barely clinging on to Na Saoirsí. And no one's bothered fixing it up.
Theo always hides out here when he's feeling wronged.
Could he have left the city?
...I've only seen one person leaving the gates—Emma from Lagran Street. Theo sure wasn't with her.
What's more, she was taking a cartful of who-knows-what to the entrance of the forest, all by her lonesome. She met up with someone there, they took the goods, then she came back by herself.
And she didn't notice you?
The cityfolk don't see too well. Or hear. Or smell. Besides, I was pretty far from her.
So she delivered a large shipment and returned by herself... Where did all those goods come from?
What does this Emma do for a living?
Runs a grain shop. I've delivered letters to her before.
So a shopkeeper... left the city... and now keeps close at home...
Seeing how this is the only lead we have, we should hurry over to Lagran Street.
Brigid? Reed?
Hello. You wouldn't happen to have seen Theo around, would you?
Theo? Did Nemos send you to find him?
That's right.
Nope, haven't seen him. Heavens knows where that boy has run off to. I hope he didn't skip town along with the spies that set the ag plate on fire.
How can you—
Brigid. We need to find him.
How about Emma, then? Have you seen her anywhere?
She closed up shop early today. Actually, I came to see if she might be willing to reopen for me, but... I guess I wasted the trip.
*Sighs* There it is again, that cold burnt smell. It's been everywhere in Na Saoirsí lately.... I can't stand it.
I've been meaning to ask: are you able to smell purple flames?
Yeah. The scorched stench of the red dragon's flames. Lately, even the cityfolk have been reeking of it.
It's not just the smell... The fire itself has spread to them.
What? How? I don't see any flames on 'em.
So you can't see them either?
I was gonna ask the same thing. You saying you can see the flames?
Then again... *Sighs* Makes sense. You being a dragon too, and all.
The cityfolk live so close to the other one, even worship her like a god—guess it was only a matter of time before they got burned.
Emma, it's me. Brigid.
Emma!
Is she not in?
With that purple flame stench comin' out of the keyhole? Must be someone in there. So what now? Break in?
...I don't see any other options.
Whoa, so dragon-fire can burn through locks, too?
...Let's save that conversation for later. We need to get in there.
Here, Officer, have a drink. We may not have a proper feast, but there's more than enough booze to go around.
Cheers.
Oh, if it isn't our resident Wakewoman. The Leader invited you too?
I'm here... to present the sacred instruments to the red dragon on behalf of the wakemen.
Ooh, offerings from the wakemen? Let's see what you've got... a whistle? And what else?
Samhain is almost upon us. The wakemen do not have much left to offer. This is all they could spare.
That's it?
My sincerest apologies.
Weren't you the one swearing to us you'd bring in those ritual doodads to give this celebration the dignity it deserves?
I'm sorry, I didn't think it through...
Who cares? It wasn't Nemos's idea, anyway.
I was against it from the start. What's so auspicious about offerings from people who deal with ghosts and corpses all day? We should be glad they only gave us a whistle.
Of course you'd take her side—she saved your life. But we can't just laugh this off! Tell us, Wakewoman, how do you plan to take responsibility for this?
Quiet!
This was not solely her responsibility. If you truly wish to hold the wakemen accountable for disrespecting The Leader, we can revisit the matter later.
Set aside the matter of the ritual objects for now. The Leader sent me ahead to make sure everyone is enjoying themselves.
These are difficult times, so we're keeping things simple—there will be no feast for the ceremony. But feel free to go and relax until she arrives.
Not you, Wakewoman. I expect you to provide Her Highness an explanation for your failure.
Perhaps you could take this chance to curry favor with the other officials. They could put in a good word for you later.
I'll... pass on that, thank you.
Quite the righteous one, I see.
Brigadier, sir, these fellows, have they always been like this?
Ha. Always?

The Bureaucrat once exhausted himself managing logistics for the Silverrock Bluffs front. He went days without sleep, until he was so worn out that he nearly collapsed on the Na Saoirsí agricultural plate.
The Cruel Officer is a cold-hearted scoundrel, to be sure. But during the war, the wealthy merchants and nobles hoarding grain near Na Saoirsí would tremble at the mere mention of his name.
The Torturer I've seen take pleasure in others wailing and screaming. But I've also seen him charge into the enemy ranks like a rabid tuskbeast.
Now, though? Had Her Highness not vetoed it, they'd probably be enjoying a lavish banquet right now, even with the city as it is.
*Sighs* Will our dear Brigadier ever remove that stick from his arse?
You look like you're in a bit of a mood too, Bureaucrat. What's wrong?
What's wrong is that the city's almost out of grain, and I'm responsible.
Well, what can you do? It was a poor harvest.
(*whispers*) Besides, didn't I tell you to pin it on the Victorians? That Emma girl said exactly what you told her to, and the townsfolk took the bait, so what's the problem?
(*whispers*) Even if there is a famine, these people treat The Leader like a god. They'd never turn on us, never.
(*whispers*) Or are you afraid Emma will spill the beans?
(*whispers*) No. She's been on edge since she got back, but she follows instructions to the letter. I'm not worried about that.
(*whispers*) What worries me is the merchant who met with her—there's been no word from him. Normally, we'd have heard something by this morning.
(*whispers*) So there's a little delay, what's the big deal?
What are you two muttering about over there?
Oh, uh...
We were discussing that hero, Culann.
Oh? What about him?

At the time, he told us he took out a whole squad of Victorian scouts in the Dark Woods by himself. Four or five men, by using the terrain to his advantage. Said there was one child left behind, but he couldn't bring himself to finish the job. Sound about right?
But think about it. Why would a scout squad bring a child with them?
Huh? The Wakewoman already explained that. The scouts had taken hostages—a group of merchants who'd travel between Tara and Victoria—and forced them to guide them through the forest.
Sure, I buy that there was a caravan, but the scouts? *sighs* I don't know about that...
'but the scouts? I don't know about that...'
His throat getting dry, the writer grabs his mug, only to find it devoid of beer.
I'll go get you some more.
The mysterious woman stands up and heads to the bar. The writer leans back in his chair.
As has been his habit for many years, he sets about planning how to make the next part of his story captivating. However, as he thinks it through, a sudden tightness grips his heart.
Even if this woman is also a survivor, and even if she knows a thing or two about what happened, he cannot simply lay everything out there like he has been, at least not for this next part.
Heavens knows what she would have to say about it.
This is just a story. A shadow cast on the wall, not the fire itself. A storyteller must always remember this, or they will find themselves consumed by the flames.
Here you are, your beer.
Thank you kindly. Now where were we?
The Dublinner's ugliness at the celebration banquet.
Oh, right.
Well then. The true star of the party is about to make her entrance...
The true star of the party is about to make her entrance.
But until then, Dublinn's ugly visage will remain, so hideous that no one can face it directly.
*sighs* Well, I—
Tch.
What is it?
Is there something crawling on my head?
Ha. It may be freezing here in Na Saoirsí, but at least there are still bugs. Don't move. I'll grab it.
W-Wait...
The banquet hall suddenly quiets down.
Everyone is staring at The Bureaucrat's head, where a beetle is perched.
A solid gold beetle.
...Uh, one of the decorations fell down. Go on, put it back.
The Officer removes the gold beetle from The Bureaucrat's head and places it in his palm.

The Bureaucrat lets out a sigh of relief. He is about to place the beetle back in its original spot when it flutters its diamond-encrusted wings, flies a short distance towards the door, and lands in the center of the hall.
He takes a few steps forward, and the beetle, as if tempting him, crawls a little further ahead.
Such enthusiasm, Mr. Bureaucrat!
Your Highness?!
I-I just wanted to catch this stray beetle for you!
Of course. Why would I blame you for that?
O-Oh, alright then.
By the way, about the sacred instruments I reported to you about...
A trifling matter.
What is truly important is that my faithful servants be rewarded for this victory.
Eblana picks up the beetle from the ground and flattens her palm. The beetle buzzes as it flies toward the door.
Ladies and gentlemen, we are in the very hall where the treasures of the ancient King Gaeil were once displayed.
The furnishings had all been looted by the Victorians, as they are wont to do, but we have finally returned most of them to their rightful places.
That beetle you were chasing was awarded personally to a capable official. He was praised for his tireless work in gathering ever last grain of the harvest and storing it in the treasury.
And now, you are worthy of the same. Without you, our army would likely have collapsed from hunger a hundred times over.
Go chase it down, for it is yours. As long as it is willing to land on you, you will always have the right to collect our grain.
Yes... Thank you!
Now then...
Eblana scans the room.
Some stare intently at The Bureaucrat's back, wondering what rewards they will receive, while others cannot help but glance at the other treasures in the room.
The sword that enforces the law, the cloudbeast that listens to appeals, the knot that records history, the crystal teardrops that symbolize compassion...
It seems none of you can stand to wait any longer.
How about this, then: everyone, feel free to take whatever in this room you desire.
In an instant, it is as if all the treasures come to life, moving on their own.

The cloudbeast statue starts running, the crystals start rolling, the knot begins to twist, and even the ceremonial sword studded with Sargonian jewels breaks free from its stand, hopping comically toward the door.
Go, my servants. These are my rewards for your loyalty.
It is unclear who takes the first step, but once he does, the tide is unstoppable.
The crowd chases after the treasures, surging out of the banquet hall.
A beetle flies through the dark alley.
It flaps its wings furiously, but only manages to find its way into an abandoned block that few souls tread anymore.
Its golden body is too heavy, weighed down even further by the gems on its wings. It can only fly a short distance before coming to a rest in a gap between the stone tiles—
Found you!
I got it! I got it!
You've got your bug, now help me get these crystals before they fall into the ditch!
Coming—
Hold on, I am going to get my cut if I help you, right?
Sure, sure! Just hurry! You don't want to see these crystals end up in the ditch, do you?
Sweet cloudbeast, you're mine!
Good... all the gems on the sword are there...
Where are The Brigadier and the others?
Who cares?
The Bureaucrat tucks away the parasite, while The Officer eagerly watches the crystal tears drop again and again.
The Ombudsman brusquely manhandles the cloudbeast, while The Torturer raises the sword as though it conferred power over life and death.
What a lively scene.
All of this treasure is yours, my servants.
I am not so miserly as to withhold them after seeing how much you love them.
But what more do you intend to take from me, once I have given you these?
Let me guess...
A path appears at the dead end of the alley, and even more shiny baubles rush towards it.
There is no time to bother with what is already in their hands.
They scramble for the treasure on the ground, stuffing it into their pockets, carrying it on their backs, and rushing towards the next shiny piece before even standing back up from the last one.
None of them care where the path leads.
...going to Dossoles tomorrow... It's high time I treated myself to something nice...
...squeeze these country bumpkins in Na Saoirsí a little more...
...don't forget my share...
...can't wait for the townsfolk to send me some 'Victorian spies'...
My, my. After far more than just wealth, aren't we?
A life of luxury. A high status. Power over life and death. You want it all.
I have nothing against being true to your desires. And what you desire are things that I will not miss.
Unfortunately, you happen to be my servants, yet you seem far greedier than I.
So goodbye. Go to where you belong.
The treasures pave a shiny path where none existed, sending the madmen out of the Lair and out onto the land...
Towards whence the darkness came, the gaping mouth of the Dark Woods.
...This part is very different from the rest of your work.
I told you, I've abandoned many drafts in the process of observing, thinking, and writing. Any and all of them ready to be thrown in at a moment's notice.
Do you really think I'd be sitting here, drinking this swill, if I could tell this story as a consistent, unified narrative?
You're the one insisting it gets a proper ending, not me.
True.
But if I must regard this passage as your own personal embellishment, should I also consider the rejection of Brigid by the mist to be a creative liberty as well?
...No more than the Exiled King you forced into the story.
Then what about the rest of the story you are about to tell? Should it be seen as an observation, or as a creation?
That's up to you to decide. Do you want me to tell it or not?
Of course, of course. Go on...
So what was wrong with Emma?
Just a cold, on top of mild malnutrition.
She didn't look so bad on the outskirts of the Dark Woods.
...A sickness of the spirit, most likely.
Iron for the statue needed to be collected from door to door. People won't even turn on their lamps to save fuel, never mind food...
What could she possibly be shipping out?
She runs a grain store. Maybe...
But only a grain store. She couldn't have done this on her own. There must be somebody behind her.
We also still need to ask about Theo. There's no time to wait for her to come to on her own.
Ugh—agh!
Can you hear me, Emma?
Ni—Niels!
Take me with you... take me with you!
No, she's still dreaming.
Niels... is that somebody's name?
I've seen it written on letters she's gotten. A loved one, if I had to guess.
And where might we find them?
No idea. It's been a while since the last letter.
Niels...
Scold me, beat me if you must... but don't abandon me...
The Bureaucrat told me to smuggle food, so I did... What meaning is there in a life without you? What point is there in being kind to others?
The Bureaucrat... Dublinn.
There's so much food in the city. A single cart won't make a difference...
There's no food in the city. A single cart won't make a difference...
Won't make a difference...
Should we keep trying to wake her up?
Wait.
...Why wasn't it you who lived?
Those accursed Victorians... Why did their child live?
A child... of Victorians?
Why...
Ugh... uh...
It'll be alright, my dear—I'll make things right—
Wait for me, Niels... Wait for me, my love...
I can't see you, my love! I can't see you...
Why? Why?!
The Victorians who killed you have been chased out!
The last Victorian bastard died... before my eyes...
Niels...
Sweat fills Emma's fevered brow. Tormented by nightmares, she instinctively grabs the sheets beneath her, but it does not stop her from trembling.
The other two stand up at the same time.
It's already past curfew. Will we really able to move freely?
Stick with me. If the mist wants to take either of us, let it try.
Wait, someone's coming over from the citadel!
Who is it?
Looks like Dublinn. It's...
The Brigadier!
What's going on? It's like he's a walking ghost...
Brigadier!
Who's there?!
Loughshinny?
Go back, now, back to...
What happened?
No.
Nothing happened tonight.
We have a lead on the food shortage, sir.
The owner of this grain store was instructed by The Bureaucrat to smuggle food out of Na Saoirsí. We may be able to get it back, if we're quick.
The Bureaucrat? I should have known—
But it's too late. Everything's too late.
Why? Do you know where the food went?

Not the food...
Me. Us.
Thank you for informing me, but I...
What is going on? Tell me!
No, nothing happened tonight.
I can't imagine what could have turned you... a warrior with no qualms about killing for the greater good, into... this.
Did you and Dublinn found this nation over the corpses of so many, just to starve your own people to death here?
No. Never.
Thank you for informing me. I will... deal with it, personally.
But do not mention the name Dublinn ever again.
You got your promise. Do not set foot on the streets again tonight, and especially do not break curfew with someone insignificant, as you're doing now.
This is for your own good.
Loughshinny opens her mouth to speak, but The Brigadier shows no acknowledgment.
He turns around and walks towards the outskirts of the city with heavy, dragging steps.
Are we gonna do as he says? Hide?
...No, Nemos needs to know about Theo. Do you know where she lives?
It's a wee bit far, practically the other side of Na Saoirsí.
Then that's where we're going.
Nemos's house is just ahead.
That was easy. Seems the mist didn't get into the city tonight.
Nemos? Are you home?
Nemos?
Hey, didn't she say she'd be at the Dublinn victory party? Is she not back yet?
The Brigadier was leaving the citadel. The party should be over.
Miss Loughshinny... Brigid?
Loughshinny is about to speak when her gaze meets Nemos's.
Her eyes are even blanker than the Brigadier's were.
What's going on? Did something happen at the victory party?
No, nothing happened tonight...
What are you two doing here?!
Quick, inside! You mustn't be out on the streets tonight!
As Nemos hurries Loughshinny and Brigid inside, The Brigadier passes beyond the boundaries of Na Saoirsí.
He walks into the forest, where there is nothing but darkness, towards the depths of the dark mist.
Then, the food carts that had been smuggled out of Na Saoirsí slowly emerge from it.
They will return to Na Saoirsí's agricultural plate before dawn.
The next day, the city gets the smallest of reprieves from the food shortage. No one notices that The Brigadier never returned from the Dark Woods.
The food mysteriously appear at the agricultural plate before making its way to the grain stores across the city. It falls well short of truly solving the problem.
But it was far more than just The Brigadier who never came back, though the people of the city do not know it yet.
Once again the sun rises, without pity or care.
The dragon and the wayward wakewoman walk the streets, watching with shock at how the now-commonplace brawls descend into even more brutal violence.
The nomad knocks on the blacksmith's door. She brings woeful news.
Ouch!
The hell's wrong with you, Theo? You have eyes, use them!
S-Sorry, Mr. Rory, I was just hoping to try my luck over at Lagran Street, see if I could get some potatoes—
Get lost!
Sorry...
Look out! The burdenbeast's panicking! Out of the way!
Stupid coachman! You nearly ran me over! Get back here!
That dung-for-brains ran off, just like that?!
Mr. Rory, I...
Hah, look who DID get run over! Serves you right.
You're not going anywhere now! Let's go see the Dublinn folks. Maybe they'll give me potatoes for handing over a traitor—
Better stick to the back streets. Can't have someone else taking my spy...
The man grabs the boy's collar and drags him into a small, empty alley. The boy groans in anguish.
Weird. Did I forget the way?
Gimme back my potatoes! Go and buy your own! They still have some at the shop!
Give them back!
They still have potatoes?!
The man glances towards the voices, then at the broken body of the boy whose arm he is still grabbing.
He's dying. Would the Dublinners even give me anything for a dying kid? Could they even get anything out of him?
...It's your lucky day, traitor.
The man shakes off the boy's hand, which had been grabbing his sleeve, and walks off briskly towards the voices.
My leg... it hurts...
I can't stand...
Mrs. Emma...
Wh-Who's there? Who's calling my name?
I-I did as you asked! Just leave me alone—
...Emma!
Theo? What happened?
I got hit by a carriage, then left behind here.
I'm not a Victorian spy, Mrs. Emma... Please, help me... take me to Mr. Culann...
A Victorian... spy?
Ah... it's all happening so fast...
It hurts so much...
I know the Victorians did bad things. Nobody likes me, nobody listens to me, nobody wants to get close to me.
But could you at least... find Mr. Culann... it hurts... it's so cold...
When I grow up... I'll find the Victorians and take revenge... for Mr. Niels...
(Takes a deep breath)
