Maybe I'd have helped you, Theo, if Niels was the only one who died.
Mrs... Emma?
You know that Niels died in the war, but even more people died without ever seeing the battlefield.
You Victorians blocked off the supply routes. You still are. So many people starved. Including me, including...
Including the child Niels left behind... the child inside me.
He starved to death because of you Victorians.
Find someone else to get Culann for you. They're all good people, unlike me...
...Very good people.
Emma. How could you do that to him, Emma...?
What could have changed you so much?
I'm not defending her, Culann, but she wasn't the only one there that day.
No, she wasn't...
Sorry, shouldn't have said that.
No, you're absolutely right.
You wanted to take Theo with you, didn't you? What did you see?
He was being bullied. And I'm not talkin' squabbles between kids... by adults.
I intervened the first time I saw it. I thought... I thought it wouldn't happen again.
But it did. A lot.
I had a bad feeling that he didn't belong here, that there was no place for him in Na Saoirsí. But I never thought...
I understand. You should go.
Culann?
I said I understand.
You...
I SAID I UNDERSTAND! NOW GO! GET OUT! LEAVE ME—
...Leave me be for a while. Please.
...I'm sorry.
The blacksmith stands silent in the room. All is quiet save for the crackling of the fire.
Theo, I...
Too many things have passed him by, too many things he could not understand. He no longer has the strength to find out why, to separate cause from effect.
The only thing he knows is that he never achieved anything.
What kind of hero am I?!
Yeah. What kind of hero are you?
The blacksmith looks around him. There is no one else in the smithy, save the empty eye sockets of the dragon statue, now glaring at him accusingly.
What kind of hero are you?
What... what kind of hero am I?
What kind of hero would leave a child to the mercy of abuse and ill-will?

What kind of hero would allow food to be smuggled out of the city under his very nose, until there is nothing left. All while doing nothing more than handing out a few shriveled potatoes to his neighbors?
What kind of hero would shut himself in his smithy while the city descends into bedlam, all to satisfy his own pathetic vanity by etching his name into a pedestal?
No!
I-It's not out of vanity. All I wanted was to use the title of 'hero' to... to...
'To bring happiness to our countrymen who lost everything in the war,' as you told Nemos?
Like now?
Like... now?
The weak you swore to protect are dead and gone.
The justice you swore to uphold has fallen into darkness.
The people you swore to bring happiness to... Emma and Rory... are they even your people anymore?
Or are they the dead, living in the past?
The dead... living in the past?
Your heart still dreams of the future, vain as it is. But them? They are the walking dead, stuck in the past.
Who is it you are calling your countrymen? The wretch who spends his days brandishing an antique sword he salvaged from the flames?
The smuggler who let an innocent child die because she miscarried her own?
'Townsfolk' who no longer know anything but hate in the face of change?
Go on, go out and find someone, anyone. Ask them what their plan for the future is. What do you think the answer will be?
The blacksmith's hammer falls to the ground with a loud crash.
He glares at the dragon's head.
No, no...
It's you who's trapping them in the past...
I collected the plates and bowls my people used day to day, lit the fuel they relied on for warmth and light, just to feed you... you...

You monster.
I don't know about the food. But the metal and the fuel...
It was me. I fed you those pots and pans, I put the people's livelihoods into your mouth with my own hands, all so that you could grow and grow...

You've devoured everything in my life, then you devoured Theo himself...
Theo... Theo...
It was me, I nurtured the monster that took your life!
Soon it will stand in the square on Samhain, spread its wings, and turn everyone in the city into the dead, those who can only live in the past...
You're right, monster. I'm no hero.
I'm a fraud. A vain, pathetic fraud. Once, I fooled others; now, the only one I'm fooling is myself.
But they need me.
They need you?
They need a hero to wake them up, to tell them that the war is over, that what really matters is what lies ahead. The future. Tomorrow.
And you think you're the one?
I'm the one.
The blacksmith walks away from the forge with resolute steps. He takes the thickest metal chain he can find and walks back to the dragon, its empty eyes still fixated upon him.
Come with me.
Where to?
To the square.
It's not Samhain yet.
I can wait no more. I will not wait for your ominous prophecies to come true.
Come with me...
I will dismantle you...
And return our lives to the people.
The dragon does not move. In the eyes of the blacksmith, however, he sees the dragon bow its head, perhaps mockingly, perhaps invitingly.
Reed, Nemos, I'm back. Culann's in a real bad place right now. You should go see him.
How about on your end? What's going on in the city?
People are still looking for spies... Victorian spies.
Are they off their rockers? Taran or Victorian, we're all human. We gonna keep fighting to the death like this, when the war's already over?
Tell them, Nemos. Tell 'em that Dublinn made 'em go hungry, and was punished by the Red Dragon. The food's now—
Do not mention the word Dublinn ever again, Brigid. The Leader's orders.
I... only overheard whispers about the food at the victory party. No evidence, no trial.
I don't even know how it got back.
Besides—news about all those missing people hasn't spread at all... How am I supposed to explain it?
If they can't be reasoned with, we'll do this the hard way.
What do you mean?
When I came back, I told myself I would never be her shadow again.
But if the true Leader is refusing to show her face and take responsibility, then I must step up, if only to calm the people.
What about Culann? Everyone knows him as a hero. Things would be much simpler if he's willing to help.
He should still be at his smithy. I'll lead the way.
As for you, Brigid, you should leave the city as soon as possible.
Why?
Well... if we and Culann both fail, then the people will...
I see. They don't much like me here, so I'd just get in the way, is that it?
I'm sorry, Brigid, that's not what I meant, but—
But it's true.
Don't worry, I'll try my luck in the Dark Woods. Sixth time's the charm.
Culann!
Culann...
Is he not in?
He...
What the...?!
They are both rendered speechless.
The door of the smithy...
No, the entire front wall of the smithy is gone.
The wall, which was supposed to stay up until the statue was finished, is now a pile of rubble. Visible through it is a long trail leading to the square.
Along with drag marks so broad they span almost the entire breadth of the road.
Hundreds, thousands of years ago, the Tarans' ancestors did the same as their descendants do now, lighting bonfire after bonfire in the barrens between autumn and winter.
They would sleep beside the red dragon, their red-hot dreams buffeted by sand and wind until raw, but even so it was enough to keep everyone warm.
They would wake up to a still-burning bonfire, because the red dragon does not sleep.
With a moment's rest, the red dragon could find respite from fatigue and pain by breathing in the dreams of homeland, liquor, and freedom rising slowly from the flames and into the sky.
Today, Samhain approaches. The people fuel the flames with wood torn from nearby ruins, and have entirely forgotten the ancient leisure time that seemed to last forever.
Put your hand in the fire.
Look at the color of the flames on my hip. I'm a Taran, I tell you, one blessed by the Red Dragon.
You think a spy couldn't fake the color of a lamp? Get on with it!
May...
May the Red Dragon be merciful!
Oh... it... doesn't even hurt too bad.
Really?
Look. You can barely make out the burn marks on my hand.
So you're not a Victorian spy. But do you know of anyone else who hasn't undergone the trial yet?
You. Yes, you! Have you burned your hand over there yet?
Say something, will you? Enough fiddling with that broken knot of yours! Come with me!
I'll take you there! Just put down the knot—
You—You hit me?!
My knot... you dared...
Fine, keep your bloody knot. Just come with me to see if you're not a Victorian spy!
The quiet resident stands up without a word, and heads towards the bonfire with shaky steps.
The square has already descended into complete chaos.
Though half of it stems from suspicion and hatred—not to mention frustration at having revealed no spies yet—the other half comes from the looks of despair and the deathly silence.
Until a faint sound of something dragged across the ground rumbles in the distance.
The sound grows louder and louder, until it drowns out all the clamor and chaos.
Everyone hears the sound of something massive slowly being dragged closer to them.
In a flash, every other sound fades, leaving only the shaking of steel and gears in its wake.
Hidden in the humid mist is the gigantic silhouette of an ice-cold statue.

Forged by a hero, and still considered incomplete by the townsfolk, the statue of the red dragon's ancestor is presented to Tara. It arrives in the raging light of the dying sun, yet even so nearly manages to shroud the sky.
The dragon statue... came to life?
No, that's—Culann?!
Culann, are you here to help us find the Victorians too?
He has to prove he isn't one first!
Over here, Culann! Listen, the fire won't hurt you as long as you're no Victorian—
Are you deaf?! Get over here already!
Hold on, he...
He dragged this giant statue all on his own?!
They finally notice the iron chains wrapped around his arms, one, two, three times around, and that the statue's weight was resting on those wide yet mortal shoulders.
The hero does not respond to his name and continues forward. The statue grinds down on him till it draws blood. His flesh is nearly rent asunder, his bones cry out in sorrow, and yet he presses on.

The young hero walks ahead of the statue, unafraid of the disparity between his small stature and the gigantic structure. He takes one step, then another, each heavy and certain, towards an open space in the crowd.
He parks the statue in the square's empty center, and struggles to turn around before facing everyone before him.
The steel skeleton bears an icy elegance, wings frozen in motion craving for flight, in place as if it were meant to be there since the beginning.
Stop, my brothers and sisters.
Before we kill any more of our own.
For a moment, no one dares to respond.
Hate and doubt both remain, as well as the pain that keeps everyone from advancing towards him like an army of walking corpses.
The man before them is a hero. He protected Na Saoirsí. He dragged both blessing and curse all the way here, and the statue behind him laces his every word with an awe-inspiring weight.
Kill our own? Who?
You are burning hands with fire now, but what if your spy isn't found?
He really dragged the statue here...
What should we do? Should we... interrupt him?
Did our food not return to us? Is the war not over?
Say there really was a spy. Why must the entire city gather here? Why must we hurt each other, just to find one lowly spy?
Let him... finish.
I'll say it again. The war is over.

When it had just ended, my thoughts were simple. I'd wait for a smith better than myself to arrive, then learn a thing or two from them so I could repair what I had made myself.
But...
No smiths came, and I learned no skills. I even took what you lot needed to live, and forged it into the statue.
That was wrong of me. Na Saoirsí is short of everything. Even an empty bowl is better put to catching rainwater than being cast into a shiny statue that does nothing but grow.

And the food. I was so worried yesterday. So afraid that famine would put us past the point of no return. Maybe it was the Red Dragon's mercy, or maybe one of the ag plates had a good harvest, but the fact is, our food is back.
And as long as we're alive, we can make it through this. Am I wrong?
But Theo... is no longer alive.
You still feeling pity for that Victorian bastard?!
Yes. I am. When I heard of his death, I began to hate everyone in this city, myself included.
You know better than any of us what the Victorians have done!
I know that with the last of their strength, they leaned over and begged me to take him away.
I hate you all. I even feel like taking revenge on any of you who sat back and watched him die.
But revenge needs a reason, doesn't it? What's our revenge for, then?
To rob them just like they robbed us back then!
And how do you suppose Na Saoirsí could manage to take even a single grain from the Victorians, when we've already melted our axes into this statue?
We have to let those bastards feel the pain we went through.
Yeah, just like how I want to make you lot feel what it's like to die alone in an alley!
Our brothers, our sisters died so we could live. Why must we now throw our lives away just to make others suffer?
Do you want us to love them, then?! To love those who stole our crops, killed our kin?
No, of course not. No one needs to love their enemy.
All I want is for you to love the living... to love yourselves.
But I don't even need you to do that, I just have one question for you all.
Tomorrow, once Samhain is over. Once the shadow of war finally leaves us... what will you do?
I wanted to finish this statue and carve my name on it. But now? I want to find a better smith to teach me to fix up a prosthetic leg.
I don't know what's up with the fancy speech all of a sudden, Culann. But I don't care what happens tomorrow, or about the war. I just want the Victorians dead.
So if you're speaking for them, I want you dead too.
Fine.
Culann swings the sledgehammer he used to forge the statue towards the dragon's head.
However, he exhausts himself both in making and moving it, so with a moment's hesitation, the hammer does not come crashing down.
The crowd even assumes it to be some sort of ritual, and for a moment does not react to his plan.
Only when they realize Culann intends to destroy the statue do even the most dreary of the living dead collectively howl and dash in his direction, surrounding him.
Don't be foolish, Culann!
Nemos's cry is drowned out by the crowd's yells, but Culann's voice remains clear for all to hear.

Loughshinny thinks of rushing over to him, but the angry crowd blocks her path. She bites her lip and tries to open a path through the sea of people with both her flames and voice, shouting to attract their attention.
Unfortunately, they act as if they have already lost their senses.
Maybe they truly have. Who can say?
I'm going to destroy this statue, in order to return the lives it took from you.
Destroy it?! How dare you!
I remember what each and every bone was forged from. I'll turn its spine back into axeheads and blades, its ribs back into bowls, its skull into tables and chairs. And give them all back to you.
Traitor! How could a hero like you betray us like this?!
If being unwilling to take someone else's livelihood makes me a traitor, then I was never a hero, but a traitor all along.
Excuses!
Excuses?
I'll tell you who Culann really is. He is no hero. He is a liar who hungered for fame.
He claimed to have killed a squad of Victorian scouts in the Dark Woods. But it was not in the woods that he met those Victorians, but somewhere further away...
They weren't even scouts. Just a band of poor traders, already trampled over by some army or other.
He wanted to save them, but in the end, could only save one boy. A Victorian child. He didn't know what to do.
Be it out of desperation or vanity, he decided to cut their heads off, and tell his people they were scouts.
That way he'd become a hero, the most prestigious person in town. He thought he could use that status for good.
Raising the boy would become an act of mercy by the benevolent hero.
You lied to us?!
Even this poor liar knew that he was still a blacksmith, and thought he should learn his trade after the war.
But you lot...
What about you?
You—You're not Culann! You're a fake, pretending to be him!
*cough*...
Yeah, you're not Culann! He's our hero, the man of steel who even a whole Victorian squad couldn't handle! Who the bloody hell are you?!
Hah...
After dragging the statue all the way here from my workshop...
How much longer... do you expect me to live?
Fresh blood gushes from Culann's mouth and nose as if he has just uttered his last words.
He bears an almost kind and expectant gaze as he looks at his compatriots surrounding him. Hoping their faces will reveal an expression other than hate.
But he feels as if his eyes meet only a sea of purple flames. The faces behind the fire are difficult to discern, as if they are the restless dead he met in those ruins outside long, long ago.
He looks into the distance.
*cough* *cough*!
In a corner at the edge of the square he sees a tiny figure.
He did not have time to look for Theo's corpse, but now it stares at him blankly, purple flames clearly visible around it.
Theo? You too?!
*cough*...
This was... all for naught...
Culann stumbles to his feet, and picks up his hammer to swing.
The crowd stares at him with dread, fearing that the hammer will fall on their heads, but they do not back down.
Culann uses the last of his strength...
And with the utmost ferocity, he strikes the dragon's head.
As if sounding a death knell.
The hero's body collapses before the giant dragon, stunning the crowd as if they've lost something.
Loughshinny's voice finally spreads as her fire burns like a sunset set aflame, but she fails to attract any attention.
At that very moment, the statue suddenly bursts into purple flames along with Culann lying quietly before it, sending the crowd reflexively into retreat.
Within the fire, Culann begins to pick himself up, while the giant statue extends its wings as if to fly away.
When Culann gets fully to his feet, the dragon statue flaps its huge wings, lifts off, and lands on the empty pedestal in the center of the square prepared just for it—
The crowd watches in complete silence, though whether out of shock or reverence is unclear.
From the citadel come the faintly discernible sounds of footsteps, slow and late.
Truly a shame that I missed the only hero left in this city.
Well then, people of Na Saoirsí. Your hero has sacrificed his life for this statue.
Nightfall approaches, so if you still follow the Red Dragon's will...
Then you will obey curfew and head home.
'Culann uses the last of his strength, and with the utmost ferocity he strikes the dragon's head, as if sounding a death knell.'
You're quite the fan of Culann, I see. A tragic ending, but your embellishments made it beautiful.
You flatter me.
I only ever met the blacksmith a few times. He was the first victim of the city's chaos, if you don't count Theo.
Actually, I'm rather curious about something. The Dublinners who walked into the darkness, did they also bear the 'purple flames' you mentioned?
I'm sorry, but I couldn't say.
Based on your story so far, I do not believe they would have. Corruption, smuggling, abuse of authority... They had many plans for the future.
If you've thought about it that much, you could probably come up with your own ending by now.
But that was not the only incident to occur in the city. You said so yourself: he was but the first victim.
Though, if you insist on ending this here...

lines.st: The red dragon was certain those infected by the flames of death could not be saved.
And so, she declared to her people that the land would become death's domain, and they would become her followers, accompanying her on a journey towards death.

The corpse-like masses no longer had the strength to either consent or refuse. That night, flames rose high into the sky, illuminating the fields, and since then, no one has ever seen the red dragon or her followers again.

lines.x: Ugh...
What is it?
Nothing. You can go now.
There is obviously something.
'Since then, no one's ever seen the red dragon'? Really?

The author glances towards the tavern wall, covered in newspapers no one has touched. Today's paper is there too, with a photo of The Leader gracing the headlines. It declares she is currently in Oak Grove for some ceremony or other.
Also, 'accompanying her on a journey towards death'... When did that happen?
Stories are free to go where they please.
Free, sure. But that doesn't mean we get to twist the tale beyond recognition and laugh it off.
Whyever not?
Because you and I, we're Tarans. Our stories will reflect us, no matter how distorted they become.
Oh? You still regard yourself as a Taran?
I've been a Taran since the very first time I read a line penned by Seamus Williams.
Why else would I have abandoned the warm armchairs of Castshire County's literature salon and run all the way to Tara and become a—
'You still regard yourself as a Taran?'
An extremely ominous thought flashes through the writer's mind, but he immediately rejects it.
He looks at the newspapers again. The papers report only facts, even if they are edited.
I'll just go ahead and ask. Who sent you?
I'm here under no one's orders. As you put it, I'm simply an interested reader who wishes to help you 'piece together the truth.'
Can you prove it?
Now there's a conundrum. It'd be simple enough to prove someone did send me, but the opposite? How do I prove someone's nonexistence?
The author takes another gulp of beer, despondently gripping his head. He knows it is too late to run.
But he can still turn things around. He just has to be clever about it...
I mulled it over, and you're right, it's just a story. I suppose I was a tad nervous. Apologies.
You do have quite a few unfinished tales as well.
Perhaps we can join them together, and find an ending true to both Na Saoirsí as it was, and the Tara in your heart.
So if you're ready, then for the next story, how about we talk about the dance in the mirror?
The... dance in the mirror? What?
No, this was not a matter of cleverness.
As long as he is prudent and lets the shadow of the story cast by firelight rise and warp, he will definitely be fine.

lines.st: Once there was a red dragon of pride, who snubbed all companions, talking only to herself in the mirror from time to time.
Once, at Samhain, she was to host a grand banquet and her mirrored self was to help. But the two could not appear together before the guests, or the reflection would perish.
The reflection had no choice but to obey the dragon's will. She went and fetched the wakeman for the banquet. The wakeman rejected their invitation and told the reflection to desert.
Still, the reflection returned to the lair on her own, only to find the banquet had already begun. The reflection hurried to hide in darkness beyond the bonfire.
As the banquet went on, the guests somehow began to vanish, one by one. And the red dragon found and dragged her mirrored self out of the darkness, forcing the reflection to dance with her.
The two kept dancing, so relentlessly that the reflection could not feel her toes. She asked for a little rest, but the red dragon was never satisfied.

lines.x: Here, Loughshinny.
The book you wanted. The sequel to the Steam Knights adventures.
'Why', you ask?
Do I need a reason to do something for my beloved sister?
But, do you really need to read it?
Do you really need to read a book you have no interest in at all, but feel pressured by others our age to read?
I do want to read it.
Didn't you tell Father you didn't want anyone asking you why a Victorian wouldn't enjoy stories about the Steam Knights?
This book... did Dad ask you to buy it for me?
Why do you ask?
Because... how did you know?
You're backing away from me, Loughshinny.
I don't want you to be scared of me like everyone else.
No, I...
If me knowing what you told Father is so scary, how about we dance together to calm you down? Hm?
A... dance? Really?
Why not?
With those words, Eblana gracefully extends a hand towards her sister.
But in Loughshinny's memories, even when her parents put on their favorite music, even when Father shook his head to the melody, even when Mother tapped her fingers to the beat...
Her sister would only sit perfectly still while bearing an icy smile.
She had never moved her body to any tune. Not even once.
Once you can keep up with me, you'll no longer have to worry about any book, request, or even friends our age.
The unopened knight novel falls to the ground, and the pleasant new book smell is now drowned by muddy water.
She was still young then. She never imagined that achieving her desires could feel so heavy and bitter. She only remembers stumbling, turning away, ignoring what was reaching out to her...
A soft and cold hand.
Loughshinny.
The crowd is gone. You need no longer hide your identity.
Do you still think of them as alive, even now?
I only know that every Taran's heart burns with a similar flame. And that ever since our ancestors first bent the knee to the Victorians, that flame has never gone out.
That goes for all the townsfolk, as well as those who followed me back here. They all have the same fire, except that theirs has never been tainted by the shade of death.
What a wonderful analogy, Loughshinny.
Then let me ask you again: what will you do?
Dye these flames in your own hue? I imagine you must have tried already.
No matter who they are, once tainted by death, your flames can only render them into ash.
So I have a proposal, Loughshinny. Kill me.
And everyone in the city as well?
If it bothers you how this will be recorded in history, then you can just say that I killed them, and that you merely laid them to rest.
The people of Na Saoirsí are still alive.
Even if what animates their bodies is not life, but death?
If they were truly devoid of even the faintest spark of life, they would have long since become one of those hollow, shambling corpses you have wandering about.
Oh, they will. Eventually.
No, they won't.
I'd rather see the purple flames in them as a plague, one instigated by you.
And you plan to find a cure for it?
That's right.
I want to cure you of it as well, Eblana.
How wonderful.
...Wonderful?
Yes. That's absolutely wonderful.
Did you not notice?
You aren't trying to run from me anymore. You're trying to cure me, of all things.
Come, dance with me, Loughshinny. The same dance I've always invited you to, the one you always ran from.
What... did you say?
I asked you to dance with me.
But before the final bar, be sure to pierce my heart with your spear. This plague is lethal, and there is no cure. I do not question your resolve, but your efforts will be in vain.
You've run from your fate long enough... From Na Saoirsí's fate, and ours too.
It is not yet Samhain, but the boundary between life and death is more ambiguous than it has been in all of Taran history.
Do you truly wish for Tara to become a kingdom of death?
Loughshinny subconsciously turns her head away.
The fierce purple flames still rage in the skeleton the hero dragged all the way here. Culann, now taken over by purple flames, briefly stands up before sinking back down to kneel.
She cannot bear another look at him, so she turns her gaze back to her sister's eyes—and the hand she's been offered for so many years.

It is unclear if it is her imagination or a side effect of the purple flames depriving everything they touch of vitality, but the hand before her seems so pale and bloodless compared to the one in her memories.
We speak of a city and the lives of countless people, Eblana. You cannot sum this up as a matter as simple as 'fate'.
Are you finished? I believe we've spent enough time talking past each other.
...Eblana!
Oh, but feel free to continue. If you so desire, I shall wait here for as long as you need me to.
But do try not to take too long. You can afford to wait, yet that may not be true for others.
(Breathes deeply) You're right. You can afford to wait, but that may not be true for others.
Alright. I'll dance your dance. Until one of us falls and can dance no more.
But I will not follow your lead. Absolutely not.
As you please.
Though you seem to have misunderstood something, my dear Loughshinny.
The one who cannot afford to wait is standing before you now.
'Cannot afford to wait'?
Loughshinny scrutinizes her sister's face as she suddenly realizes something entirely unrelated to their conversation.
The offered hand is indeed pale, and it is not just her imagination.

Purple flames seem to weave together into an omnipresent cloak, giving Eblana her beautiful yet terrifying appearance. Yet they also fail to conceal the malady which seems to consume her beneath her pale white skin.
She shouldn't be like this. She was never like this before.
Eblana, are you...
Hm?
You...
Are you... alright, Sister?
A smile spreads across Eblana's face. The same old ice-cold smile is always found there.
This time, it seems to bear some warmth, but this only highlights her conspicuous paleness.
I appreciate your concern. Really, I do.
After all those apathetic partings and painful reunions, to only now hear you asking if I'm well... It has truly been so long.
Incidentally, I really do want a dance with you.
...I will, once I find a way to heal you.
Very well. Then I shall await you here.
Eblana strides towards the citadel, her footsteps so faint that they seem almost frail.
Phew...
Hoo... Red dragon! Nemos!
There was no one at the gates when I got back! I thought something must've happened! What's going on in the square—
Red dragon... what's that...?
Loughshinny is holding a small box in her arms. The dim night lights seem to make it harder than usual to see.
I couldn't save Culann. I...
This was the only way for him to rest in peace. So he'd no longer have to suffer from the flames in the square.
The nomad is silent for a long time, until she finds a stone on the ground.
She hurls it with all her strength towards the citadel.
But the darkness of the night swallows it up, and it fails to even make a sound.
It's already past curfew. Nemos, Brigid, let's just—
No...
No.
Nemos reaches out to the wooden box, as if trying to brush nonexistent dust off the lid.
She suddenly recalls being rejected by her own people a few days ago, and the superfluous words she heard then.
The wakemen do not stand with the living, nor do we require their reverence. Until, of course, they are dead.
That is the tradition Dublinn should be honoring.
Still, I'm grateful you were at least willing to give me this whistle... I'll be going now.
Wait, Nemos. You knew well that Samhain approaches and the wakemen would be lacking sacred instruments. Yet you still came.
Did the Dublinners force you to?
No, I came here of my own—
Nemos, you haven't changed one bit.
You wait, mediate, and reconcile... Are you also like this in Na Saoirsí? Do you also serve the Red Dragon like this?
I just... don't want to see a situation that could be improved deteriorate any further. Or people who could have cooperated fighting each other.
If conflict is inevitable, you will only ever sink deeper into the mire.
The city has tainted you with its darkness, Nemos.
So, The Leader—the Red Dragon—
Eblana will never give Na Saoirsí true life? Is that right, Loughshinny?
She said she couldn't. She... actually doesn't like to lie, unless there are other circumstances...
She's The Leader of Tara, the one and only Red Dragon, what could possibly be tying her hands?!
What's wrong, Nemos? You seem... different...
I get it. I finally get it, Your Highness Loughshinny.
It seems that that fire can in fact infect the living.
Nemos?!
The people I once depended on turned out to be a swarm of selfish parasites.
The Leader I once believed in turned out to be an evil dragon who doesn't care if anyone else lives or dies.
The people I once put my life on the line to protect... became the reason my closest friend died.
I can't even imagine what they would have done to him if that wicked dragon hadn't lit him up with her flames.
Culann was right to ask what we would do once Samhain is over. What should I do?
Other than revenge, there's nothing left for me either.
As she utters these words, Loughshinny sees a flame ignite within the final Na Saoirsían left untainted by the purple fire.
Your Highness, will you regard me the same as the rest of the residents—a servant of Eblana?
No. This deadly disease... It has ultimately infected even the last person in the city.
If so, Your Highness, then I swear to you that I will aid you in this dance that's been forced on you. Until the very end...
Until the wicked dragon falls.
...I have heard your vow.
Now, I must leave the city for a while. Come with me, Nemos.
To the wakemen?
Mhm. I have no hope of curing the flame here, so I thought we should seek aid from anyone who might be able to help.
No, I can't go with you. I have no right to be there anymore.
I'll stay in the city. As long as the Red Dragon doesn't realize what's going on and tear me apart... I'll try my best to keep the situation from deteriorating.
Though, I'd appreciate it if you could return this whistle for me.
And... Culann too. Please give him a proper funeral.
A proper funeral requires eulogies from friends and family, so... I'll write one. Could you read it for me there?
...Alright.
Here. It's done.
I'll go with Reed. The Dark Woods aren't lettin' anyone in or out, but it should be smoother with us two.
No, Brigid. You should stay too. I need your help.
Reed?
I'll be fine by myself.
That works, but... Nemos, you're so different now.
...I guess this is what vengeance does to a person.
Was that another trick of the shadows? This isn't the right way. I'm going in circles.
I can't see the sky. How long has it been...? How far do I still have to go?
Well, I'm in the Dark Woods, at least. The mist and noise Brigid was talking about didn't keep me out.
Tricky fae, shadows pointing the wrong way, fowlbeasts that trip you with branches... That's all more or less the same as when I was bringing Moran and the others back.
The trees are too tall, and the leaves too thick. They block out the sky, making the life here different from outside.
...This is the right path. I'm at the heart of the Dark Woods now.
The scenery seems different from before. Where did the red and gold of autumn go?
Why does it look like this?
The black mist?!
So the woods are rejecting me after all?
Loughshinny grabs her weapon tightly. If Nemos was right, and Dublinn never returned after leaving the city, then they were most likely swallowed by the mist.
But it shows no sign of aggression. It simply lingers.
Does it... reject me?
No, it's closer to indifference... disappointment?
Why?
There is no answer except the rustling of leaves.
Loughshinny shakes her head and cautiously goes around the mist, choosing a path that looks like it has a light at the end. The mist remains indifferent to her passage.
The fae that delight in leading travelers astray are nowhere to be seen.
Another dead end...
No time to waste here. I'm sorry, but I'll have to burn a way through—
You're overthinking it.
Who's there?!
Take a guess.
You... how could it be?
You're that gentleman from the city... I wanted to ask you something, but you—
Indeed, hardly the most amicable of partings. Since then, I've been following and watching you for a while now. Consider it my way of reflecting on myself.
I'll admit you're not quite what I thought. But this is no time for small talk.
Do not burn the trees. It should be enough to illuminate them with your fire. See for yourself what they really are.
'What they really are'...?
The leaves are razor sharp. The brambles are hard, and the branches have a metallic gleam... what is this?
That's not important. What's important is that your fire can't burn through it.
Now follow me.
Hurry, before the black mist changes its mind!
We can talk later, but if that thing is in a bad mood, there won't be anything left to bury when it's done with you.
Isn't that a dead end?
Why do you put so much faith in what you see?
Just listen and move!
This is... the path I took when we came to Na Saoirsí!
So this is where those trails lead?
If you don't mind me asking, are you and the forest fae—
Don't be ridiculous. I have nothing to do with them.
The woods, though... Centuries have passed, but it's hardly changed at all, other than the black mist.
Ruins the view, really.
We'll be out of the Dark Woods soon. Now could you—
Tsk, tsk, this is really...
What's happening?
Look behind you.
Reed!
Reed! We're here!
The refugees appear on the well-lit edge of the woods.
What happened in the city? It's like the black mist got angry the moment you entered it, cutting it off from the outside. It's been days since anyone went into the Dark Woods.
A few people were hunting on the outskirts when they heard sounds from within, and saw what looked like fire.
Whenever something similar happened before, the wakemen told us not to approach the woods.
This time, though, we recognized your fire. We could hardly ignore it.
You shouldn't have come. It's dangerous here—
Then you shouldn't have either, not on your own. We all learned our lesson about letting you go off alone after waiting for you in the marshes outside Londinium.
We trust you. So please, trust us back.
...Thank you.
You're welcome. Let's go back to camp.
Mhm.
Sir, would you—
Who are you talking to? Is there someone else with you?
The red dragon turns around, but her uninvited companion has already vanished without a trace, like the dew under the morning sun.
Thank you for providing refuge for these people.
Nemos entrusted them to us. We simply did our part.
On behalf of the wakemen, I promise that we will not reveal your existence to anyone unaware. And you have seen with your own eyes that your people are safe. Now it is time for you to leave.
What do you mean? Is she not welcome here?
Neither are your people, Moran.
Whenever two red dragons meet, endless conflict awaits. If you insist on involving yourselves in it, then we can no longer offer you sanctuary.
How could you?
This is how we have always been. The wakemen neither start nor end conflicts.
The disputes of the living are not our concern. We watch over only the souls of the departed.
If you are truly the noble and compassionate leader Moran imagines you to be, you will not drag these innocents into your battle, red dragon.
This isn't a battle between two red dragons. I came to seek your help on behalf of the people of Na Saoirsí—
Every battle has its reasons.
It's not an excuse. Reed wouldn't do that.
Excuse or not, the disputes of the living are none of our concern... A hard-learned lesson whose bitter fruits we continue to taste to this day.
Now leave.
What if I'm here to ask you to hold a funeral?
A funeral?
In that case, the wakemen will welcome you. But for that, and only that.
Now tell me the name of the departed, their deeds in life, and... present the remains.
His name was... Culann. He was a blacksmith.
Culann?!
How did he die?
The purple flames of death took both his life and his will. Not even my flames can help him now.
Then why is it that not a single denizen of Na Saoirsí has come to us to request a funeral since the city became independent?
So...
So it transpires that the first Na Saoirsían to be mourned since the end of the war was not taken by war at all.
So I weave the straw into a ring like this?
Yes. Then adorn it with these flowers, and the wreath is complete.
I remember when the farmers put a wreath on your head, praying for a good harvest next year.
...Mhm.
Feels like old times. I miss that.
Are you not happy here?
I rather enjoy it myself, but the others might find it stifling.
The life of a wakeman is almost like a self-punishment, after all. What's the word for it again? 'Asceticism'?
No alcohol, no overeating, no fancy foods—can't even laugh too loud. They wake up before sunrise and sleep after midnight, and everything in between is some ritual or other.
At least they don't expect the same from us, but there's no avoiding them when we have to help out on the farms and stuff.
I've practically forgotten how to laugh—hah.
Moran's the only one who goes out of her way to talk to them. She's learning something new every day. Might be how they tie certain knots, might be how to estimate the age of a tree.
Compared to the wakemen in the tales, though...
Your Highness?
Ma'am.
I did not expect to find you here working with them.
Reed has always been like this.
Hm.
Should we excuse ourselves?
No, I simply came to confirm the facts of the departed's life, for the purpose of the ritual.
Please, tell me. What happened to Culann?
I will tell you all that I saw, as I saw it.
I hope it makes you feel something.
I have already dismissed all the servants in the citadel. These footsteps, then...
...Your Highness.
Ah, it's you. Did you need something?
You aren't going to ask how I returned?
No, I won't. You came back, and that's all that matters, my loyal servant.
Besides, I have a fairly good idea of what sort of guest you can bring me.
Has the curfew started?
Just now.
Then you could say that my orders have been followed.

lines.st: ...failure...

lines.x: Failure?
Na Saoirsí is here. I am here. What failure?

lines.st: ...escape...

lines.x: The deadline is Samhain. Surely the black mist remembers this, even if it's hardly known for being a good listener.

lines.st: ...unsurprising...
...disappointing...
...unacceptable...

lines.x: Am I meant to understand something from these few words?
Or perhaps... you, too, prefer the directness of Arts and destruction to the imprecision of language?
No sooner do the words leave Eblana's lips than gleaming branches thrust forth from the dark mist, reaching for her throat.
At the same time, her spear penetrates the thick mist. Purple fire is faintly visible from within; she could burn it away to nothingness with a thought.
But the branches never find Eblana's throat, and her purple flames never ignite.
Samhain.
By the time the sun rises on the day after Samhain, the outcome will be plain for all to see.
So... why don't we dispense with these meaningless bluffs?
Eblana's spear still impales the mist, but the branches before her throat disappear a moment later, as do the flames at the tip of her spear.
The dark mist dissipates.
Your Highness...
You're still here.
Did you need something else? Is this about Dublinn?
There are only the two of us here. I will permit you to speak that word again, my most loyal servant.
Thank you, but there is no need.
Spectres are but spectres, even if they helped found a nation. There is no place for ghosts in the history books.
But I worry about my colleagues who stayed behind that day, as I did... Where are they? Could they mean you harm?
No need to worry. Even if there were, that is no reason for you to risk staying here.
...I saw a scene of chaos on my way back.
It was bedlam. People were fighting over the tiniest things... sometimes nothing at all. While others seemed to be stuck where they stood, like statues.
Just like the spectres who fought the Victorians with us.
Will they fade into darkness too?
This is Na Saoirsí, the Red Dragon's Lair, the capital of Tara. Anything that happens here will have repercussions across the nation.
No, it won't.
You don't sound convinced.
Your Highness... I...
At the end of the day, I have just one question.
One question I pray you will answer.
Very well. I will forgive your imprudence.
(Takes a deep breath)
Your Highness Eblana, that future...
That future you promised Dublinn, you promised Tara, that future where all Tarans could freely write their own dreams...
Was it all a lie?
The Brigadier cannot stop himself from trembling. The fear that had lurked in his heart for days overcomes him.
It is not because he fears his own end. He is not afraid of being swallowed by the dark mist like his colleagues, or of invoking the red dragon's wrath.
He fears that he will hear an answer he dare not consider.
It was true, every word of it.
Your Highness...
I have answered your question, Brigadier.
The red dragon's voice seems ethereal, feeble even, but it leaves no room for questioning.
It is the voice that has led the man to victory countless times, and so he slowly straightens his back.
Then I trust you.
Perhaps... Or perhaps you simply have no other choice.
Often have you chosen a path that seemed baffling, impossible even. But it always ends up leading us closer to our dream. Of that I have no doubt.
Such deep trust is indeed worthy of my gratitude.
However, I do not require you to maintain order within Na Saoirsí.
If you managed to return from the Dark Woods, then you should return there now.
Until Samhain has passed?
Until Samhain has passed.
Or...
'I stepped into the dark woods one morn, calling to the mists of hoar'...
Your Highness?
Do you hear the song coming from beyond the woods?
Hardly a day has passed since you left... Fine work, Loughshinny.
Is that what's been happening in the city?!
Reed, your sister... what is she planning?
I don't know.
Someone once told me that her plans would only ever be beneficial to Tara. Now, though, I can trust neither her nor the one who made that promise.
Are you still not going to help her, wakewoman?
She came for a funeral, and we will only accept her for that.
I have learned of the deeds of the departed.
Bring the knots and wreaths and come outside. The funeral is about to begin.
The people set aside their differences and stand up in silence.

lines.st: I stepped into the dark woods one morn, calling to the mists of hoar♪
I trod upon the road, heading out to war♪
No pipes a-singing, nor battle drums a-roar♪
Only the bells behind me, through dew and fog their rings do soar♪

lines.x: The wakemen sing softly, each attending to their own duty.
Some adorn the coffin with knots and wreaths.
Some lay firewood and kindling around it.
Some distribute candles to the attending refugees as they continue to sing.
Tiny lights begin to sway throughout the camp.
Loughshinny has a candle too, which she carefully lifts in front of her chest.
Excuse me...
Go ahead.
I didn't know the departed, and don't really have much to say... But where I grew up, people talked freely at funerals.
Certainly. This is how Tarans honor the departed.
Life should be celebrated, even after its end. The joy of the living is consolation to the dead. You are free to talk amongst each other.
I also made some snacks...
Please feel free to share them. We will also provide refreshments later.
However, we must request that you exercise restraint, considering that Mr. Culann died before his time, and under unfortunate circumstances.
We must also ask you to follow our instructions during the fire-returning ceremony.
Understood.
Is this how your folks hold funerals, Moran?
We didn't have all these rites and ceremonies. All we did was keep watch through the night. No clue what this 'fire-returning' thing is either.
Guess we just follow their instructions.
Have you ever been part of a ceremony like this, Reed?
(Shakes head)
I see...
You mean...
Loughshinny has found the wakemen. The hero's funeral has begun.
'I stepped into the dark woods one morn, calling to the mists of hoar; I trod upon the road, heading out to war'... Do you know this song?
You already know the answer.
I led the young into battle with it more than a few times.
Who is it? Who's singing?
Why is it that I still hear that song here at the site of the old capital, even after escaping the Dark Woods?
Or am I still stuck in the woods?!
No... no, the sound is coming from the furnace at the center of the ruins.
Such a beautiful tune...
No pipes a-singing, nor battle drums a-roar...
Only the bells behind me... through dew and fog their rings do soar...
I should go back...
Now I see... what I truly want...
Besides... do I really deserve... to escape Na Saoirsí?
Here, Emma, today's bread.
Thanks for sharing your food in times like these.
You shared with me before. Just returning the favor.
But... why do you keep staring at the bread in my hand like that?
(Mutters) Control yourself... control yourself...
(Mutters) She's Emma... the one who shared bread with you... you must help her... you must—
(Deep breath) I'm fine, Emma.
I-I just meant to say, you've been bedridden for so long. There'll be tough times ahead.
'No pipes a-singing, nor battle drums a-roar'...
Why are you singing that?
Someone's singing... in the distance...
Friends of the departed, please present your eulogy before the fire-returning ceremony begins.
Reed?
I... I have a eulogy here, written by Nemos. May I read it?
Of course. Go ahead.
'Dear Culann...'
A letter?
'Culann, perhaps it won't be long before I join you...'

lines.st: Culann, perhaps it won't be long before I join you—before I become a slave of the purple flame, before I turn to ash.
Perhaps there won't be anyone to free me from an afterlife of eternal torment.
But I've never forgotten what you said to me.
You said you wanted to be a hero. You asked me to let you become one.
You said Na Saoirsí needed a hero for the postwar days, one who belonged only to us. Neither Dublinn nor soldier.
You said that nearly everyone in the city had lost something important in the war, be it a partner, a child, or a future.
Without a hero, an exemplar to understand them and fight for their rights, one who would also set them right should they go astray...
Without that, they wouldn't have anything to hold onto when the war ends, when they have no one left to hate.
I agreed and assented to your request.
You took Theo in, and helped the people who'd lost their livelihoods.
You thought everything was on the right path, and so you proposed the statue. You became fully immersed and took on a great burden.
By the time you realized... it was too late.
Now, it is my turn to carry this burden, one too heavy for any single person to bear.

lines.x: '...Now, it is my turn to carry this burden, one too heavy for any single person to bear.'
'Farewell, Culann. Slán leat. Or should I say, Slán go fóill.'
'May we meet again...'
'in the warm flames.'
As she finishes, a flame is thrown into the kindling surrounding the coffin, igniting the firewood.
The crowd gathers around the bonfire, following the instructions of the wakemen.
Witness that the departed has found peace in flames. He shall no longer wander the mortal realm.
Place your candle as close to the fire as possible, or throw it in directly. Return the fire to the departed.
If you have anything to say to the departed, say it aloud to the flame. Be it praise or reproach, they are the memories that will console the departed at journey's end.
Once you have returned the fire, please remain to keep watch over his soul, until the fire has gone out.
Let us begin.
Slán leat. May you find rest, stranger—
deceiver and hero.
Moran bends down and places her candle by the fire. The licking flames swallow it in an instant, almost burning her hand.
Do you want to come closer to the fire, Reed?
If there's anyone here who doesn't deserve to pay respects to Culann, it would be me.
You couldn't have saved him.
Moran?
There was no place for him in the Red Dragon's Lair from the moment he made the choice to truly become a hero.
You should return the fire to him.
Ashes to ashes, dust to dust. Let the dead rest with our memories, as life goes on.
Return the fire...
Let the dead rest with our memories... as life goes on?
While Loughshinny is lost in thought, the others continue to return their fire and then sit down some distance from the bonfire, until she is the only one left with a candle in her hand.
She walks forward in silence, and tosses the candle into the blazing bonfire.
The dead are gone.
All their ambition, obsession, realization and desperation turn to fading smoke and silent ash in the flames.
So you won't go to the city, even to perform a fire-returning ceremony?
What you've described is highly unusual. We cannot simply take your word that the residents are no longer among the living.
Besides, the fire-returning ceremony is just that—a ceremony. Do not expect it to be anything more than that.
...Very well, then. You may return, wakewoman. Thank you for your help.
You intend to go back to Na Saoirsí, even without us?
Moran and the others have settled in well, and I've done what Nemos entrusted to me, so...
My work here is done.
Take care, Your Highness.
I will.
The red dragon sets out in the direction from whence she came. Everything that happened yesterday seems but a distant shadow now.
But also a tiny spark of flame.
'Return the fire'...
Have you made up your mind to go to your death?
The stranger?
Good timing, sir. May we talk? If you will help me—
Did your red dragon history lessons ever teach you about a poor wretch by the name of Labhréathach?
Excuse me?
I'll take that as a 'no'.
After returning to the palace for the first time in years, he never even had the chance to see his brother before the guards took him away and plunged a sword through his chest.
I know the story.
Then why are you so eager to follow in his footsteps, and have history remember you as a fool?
Leave. You've seen your sister, and you're still alive. In that, you've already achieved more than he ever did.
The story of Labhréathach and Ailill is not the story of me and Eblana.
...Fine. Let's say I was willing to hear you out. What did you want to talk about?
The city's fire-returning ceremony.
I just wanted to find someone to help with the preparations, but you seem to know a lot about the history and tales of Tara. So I was thinking you might be able to help.
History? Tales?
We can talk on the way. Come.
But that's not the way to the Dark Woods—
Just follow me. It worked out for you in the woods, didn't it?
...The ruins of the palace?
I've been here before. It's dangerous. The Originium-rich environment is—
You said you wanted history and tales. What better place in Tara than this *ancient Tengae expletive* palace?
The what palace?
Ah, never mind.
I appreciate your help in the woods, but I have important business to attend to. I don't have time to—
And what's this 'important business'? Dying for nothing?
Sure, you're different from your sister. That dragon's flames may be cursed, but her personality is as typical as they come. Arrogant, leaving no room for any other voice. You may be better, but—
I'm different from my sister, but she's not who you say she is.
But however different you may be, conflict is in the nature of the red dragons. Even if you were merely dragged into it.
And there is only ever one outcome. Only one may emerge alive.
One of you will die, sooner or later. So what's the hurry?
Have patience, young dragon. We're almost there.
We come here for this altar.
Altar? You mean that thing in the middle? Isn't that... the furnace?
'The furnace which fused together the weapons of every Taran soldier, and overturned the fate of the dynasty'... The furnace from which fallen soldiers were reborn.
That's what you call it today, isn't it? Far more important than just another 'altar', to be sure.
But an altar is an altar, even if it's a little bigger than the others. It was based on the ceremonial stone circle used to coronate the King Gaeil, and the palace was built around it.
Whenever an old red dragon died, this is where the fire was returned to them.
The fire... returned?
What you saw back at those wood-brains' camp. Gotta give them credit: they managed to remember all that, even centuries later.
Fire-returning wasn't just about a bonfire back in the day, though.
By the time of the old hag Máighréad, it was enough for the siblings of the heir to the title of King Gaeil to put their hands into the fire and get a few blisters.
Go back even further, though, and those siblings would have been burned alive together. Or so they say. There's no hard evidence.
Now you've gone even paler.
He flicks his hand, and a speck of purple fire flies forth from his sleeve, joining the same purple fire in the altar.
The flames breathe quietly, like an infant in its mother's embrace.
Your flame is purple too?
No, wait, you're a Draco...? Could you be—
Awfully slow, aren't you? Well, it's too late to run. Put your hand in.
What?!
You want Na Saoirsí to return its fire, didn't you? Put your hand in.
The red dragon falls silent, then stretches out her hand to touch the flame in the furnace. As expected, it is not warm, but to her surprise it does not feel cold either.
Now, I brought you here to see something—
The tragedy of Tara, the ill-repute of the red dragons... It is simply the cruel cycle of fate repeating itself, again and again.
Like your sister, I was an aberration that breathed the flames of death. It is not my intention to disrespect the dead, nor could I even if I wanted to, but I wanted to show you...
The fate of those who die upon this land.

lines.st: Is this where we should begin? Understandable. Of all recent events, it is the most significant.
Your sister was right. Those who have touched the flames of death are beyond help. Yet the fire should not have spread so readily.
What hatred did it take for the Dracos to once again sire an ill-omened aberration, the first in six hundred years?
Ah, and him.
If you ask me, he slept in far too late. Could hardly drag himself out of bed by sunset. Of course he became the sacrifice.

lines.x: ...Even you should not speak of the dead so lightly. Do not justify it as a sacrifice.

lines.st: You can only say that because you've seen too little. Those feelings will pass soon enough, once you're used to it—
See, here comes another sacrifice.

lines.x: The Victorians who killed you have been chased out!
The last Victorian bastard died... before my eyes...

lines.st: In a sense, your sister's dream did become reality. The Tarans have risen, and the Victorians have fallen. Simple. Direct. 'Satisfying'.

lines.x: Were you... one of the bystanders too?

lines.st: Don't get caught up on that, child. You know what I am.
This, I don't recognize. Explain.

lines.x: ...It was a war.

lines.st: There are always wars. Be specific. Who did she incinerate, and what land did she set her sights on?

lines.x: Victoria. Eblana wanted to take Tara back from the Aslans. This was the conclusion to that war.

lines.st: The Aslans? Those conceited bastards? But is she not facing a Sarkaz?
Never mind. The lions were the ones who stole Victoria. That's all I need to know.
I don't need you to tell me what this is, just as I can tell the expression beneath the mask.
The satisfaction and joy that only appear on a red dragon's visage in the face of certain death.

lines.x: No... that wasn't joy. It was the opposite—

lines.st: The opposite? Maybe you were simply gravely injured and started hallucinating on the brink of death.
But as I said, this's simply repeating the past. Cities may ride on wheels now, and the master of the realm has changed hands, but what's inside never changes.
What is this? Your home? Where did you learn to point your blades at each other? Who's this man?

lines.x: The Earl of Warwick. Our foster father.
Eblana said he helped Tarans see hope, a hope they could see but never reach. That all he ever did was drive thirsty travelers mad with mirages.
In his hand he held a delicate thread. On one end were the Tarans who had rediscovered their name, on the other lay the oppressors who had trodden upon them for so long.
...That thread was my sister and I.
Looking back... perhaps he was the first to be afflicted by the purple flame.
...Hah.
Not only do you sympathize with your sister, but you also know her well. No wonder you're so loath to kill her.
But I have no intention of becoming like her, nor will I simply allow myself to be killed.
These events, this history you had me recall. What are you trying to tell me?
That the history of Tara is nothing but the continuous accumulation and recombination of ruins of the past. What you have seen, I have too. Nothing new can be found within.
What I see are not ruins, but... the source of the flames.
The flames in Na Saoirsí are indeed my sister's fire, but they are also the great sorrow behind the bloodshed, left with nowhere to go.
It was the war... no, the pain, the oppression, the things taken from them, which began long before the war. That's what lit the fire in their hearts.
My sister shaped their sorrow into hatred, which scorched the fields of Tara and drove out the Victorians.
However, without vengeance to cloak the sorrow, that same fire has come back to burn them, burning away everything they have, until they are nothing but the walking dead.
A fine summary, child. But do you really think that returning the fire would solve all the city's problems?
It's the only path before me.
True. But how do you tread it?
Come. We've seen your story. Now see mine.
(Ancient Tengae) Don't cry now. It's just a cloudbeast.
(Ancient Tengae) But I raised Greyclaw from when she was a baby...
(Ancient Tengae) They say your fire is different, Daidí. You do not heal the living, but instead bring the dead back to life...
(Ancient Tengae) They don't come back. It's just an empty shell.
(Ancient Tengae) Even if it's just an empty shell... Please, Daidí, I just want to hear Greyclaw's voice again...

lines.st: And so the impulsive red dragon let his daughter's plea get the better of his judgment.
After a few months, strange things began to happen, not just to the cloudbeast, but to all the beasts of the pasture.
Burdenbeasts no longer ate grass. Cystybeasts no longer gave milk. The old refused to die, the young refused to live, and bare skeletons devoid of flesh began to roam the pastures.
As the purple fire spread through the herds, the red dragon was forced to leave the pasture with his people. He made the same mistake many times throughout his brief existence.

lines.x: The purple fire spread through the herds?

lines.st: What about it? Your own dear sister dared to burn people with that same fire. Now THAT'S crazy.

lines.x: How was the fire extinguished?

lines.st: You'll find out soon enough.

lines.x: This is... the citadel in Na Saoirsí? That same corridor?

lines.st: No. But your mistake is understandable.
We simply took a large step back in time. This is how the palace you currently stand in looked six hundred years ago.
It's incredible how stonemasons centuries hence managed to perfectly recreate it in this 'Na Saoirsí' of yours.

lines.x: So what happened here?

lines.st: Here... hah...

lines.x: Labhréathach the fool had made too many mistakes, the likes of which you have already seen. He grew weak, and felt his end coming.
On the eve of Samhain, he came to see the brother he had not seen for twenty years, bearing the finest gifts his nomadic life would allow.
He even convinced the druids of the city, who oversaw the festival's ceremonies, to take him to his brother.
Touched by his familial love, they led him into the city. But he failed to realize when his guide's silhouette, voice, and demeanor changed, not until it was too late.
He was taken to this very corridor, and—

I doubt that Ailill, who gave the order to assassinate his brother, was on that balcony on the other side of the door. For my eyes were wide open until the very end, but I never saw his fire through the gaps.
Where did it all go wrong?
Was he angry that the priests continued to treat me as royalty? Did he consider me leaving without a word twenty years earlier a betrayal?
Or was it someone else who wanted me dead, and he simply had no choice?
I'll never know.
Within this brief, swift act of violence, there was only one thing that I had long known, that I had never doubted—
A red dragon never forgives. The hatred of the dragons is even longer than the history of Tara.
But there is more I want to tell you.
After I died, Ailill ordered my coffin dragged through the city, for all to see the end that befell me.
And so purple fire raged through every pasture I had stayed at with my clan. Life turned its back on all who touched it; only death remained.

Seven days later, the priests returned my fire to me at a clearing outside the Dark Woods. And only when the fire they returned to me was extinguished did the purple flames in the pastures begin to subside.
It was only then that the last vestige of my consciousness that remained in the flames after death faded into darkness.
All living things that touched the flames, people or beasts, were turned to ash.
Your sister spoke true. The flames will continue to spread as long as she lives.
But she is a typical red dragon. She is not so noble as to give up her life to save those who have yet to survive. If she is inviting you back, then it is certainly a trap.
Run. Call for help. Yell. Find someone who is her equal to extinguish the endlessly spreading flames. That is the only way.
But what if she has already decided that I'm the only one worthy to dance with her?
What if I can't abandon the lives in the city to their fates, as long as there is any chance of saving them?
So you'll return the fire to someone who's still alive?
Yes.
And what if that doesn't work?
Regardless, I'm going back, no matter what.
Silence, and then Loughshinny hears a sigh.
(Ancient Tengae) I suppose stubbornness and stupidity really are hereditary.
This is far enough. We're already at the wakemen camp.
Your sister roused me from my slumber. You could always return me to it with your own flames, if you want.
Reed!
A golden-haired Perro leaps off the back of a burdenbeast and rushes toward the red dragon.
The burdenbeast is dragging an improvised cart with numerous long wooden boxes stacked upon it.
—Brigid?
What are you doing here? Are those—
...Coffins?!
Yeah, coffins. Nemos said to bring 'em to the wakemen. Are they still in the ruins?
Say, is there someone with you? I can smell that same burning stink, and I could swear I heard you talking just now—
Hmph.
No, I'm alone.
These are...
Dead folks.
The type who'd pull their own arms out of their sockets to bite at their imaginary enemies if you grabbed their hand. Along with the poor wretches they tore to pieces.
And Theo. Nemos found him in the end. He...
It's almost like this was the natural order of things. The bonfires will be burnin' brightest when night falls on Samhain...
The red dragon looks towards Na Saoirsí, and sees a faint shadow in the distance, painting the silhouette of purple flames.
We'll be out of coffins if this keeps up.
I hope Brigid made it out of the city. And that the mist in the woods is thin enough for her to follow the trail...
Blast it, Culann left a few unused weapons in his house. I can't let anyone see them.
And this fuel... explosives? Her Highness Loughshinny might find a use for them.
No, what's the point when every servant in the citadel has already been afflicted by the purple flames? Besides, the greedy red dragon has already sent them away.
Almost as if... she's preparing for a showdown with Her Highness.
Or... setting a trap?
Who's there?!
Rory? What are you doing here?
You're... Nemos.
Yes, I'm going through Culann's effects. If this isn't urgent, could I ask you to—
Traitor.
Have you gone mad?!
Oh, silly me. Of course you have. We all have.
Traitor—
I... I still can't bring myself to kill them. Even if they might as well be dead already.
Ha. Hahahah...
I'd better drag this fellow back home. Could do without the unwanted attention.
Phew...
Nemos?
D-Don't mind me, I'll be on my way.
What's that in your hand?
Oh, nothing. Now, if you'll excuse me—
That necklace. It was a gift from Niels to Emma.
I-I gave her bread! That's way more valuable than jewelry in times like these! She gave it to me!
Did she now? A necklace she never takes off?
I-It's none of your business!
Nemos? You stayed behind? I thought all the Dublinners left the city!
Don't tell me you're one of those living statues now, who refuse to move or talk?
I might be soon. Why did you bring them up?
Samhain begins tonight. So I went through the houses of the missing people, and found plenty of good stuff for the party tonight!
...Samhain? Party?
About Culann—well, he was already on his last legs by then, and I didn't really...
Let's not talk about that! Look at all this stuff! It'll be a night to remember!
The woman shows Nemos her bag, revealing a bottle of curded cystymilk, a few long-since-sprouted potatoes, and several bare bones.
Just wait for me, Nemos, Culann... we'll have a fantastic Samhain...
Only when the woman has already disappeared around a corner does Nemos remember who she is: a villager who once lived on the outskirts of Na Saoirsí.
Two years ago, the Victorians took her father away while investigating Dublinn. The autumn harvest had just ended, and she had been preparing to celebrate the harvest festival.
What Loughshinny hears about the situation in the city exceeds her wildest imagination.
I must go back. Now.
Wait for me. I'll come with once I've delivered these folks to the wakemen.
Are you sure about this, Brigid?
It's dangerous in the city. If I fail... there may be no survivors.
True, but I still gotta go.
Why?
Because it wasn't like this before.
The cityfolk never had much to spare, but they always had a cup of water for me when I delivered their letters.
They didn't see me as one of their own, but didn't walk away with a sneer when I told them nomad legends either. They listened.
And they definitely didn't bite, claw, and punch at people like they do these days. Like the walking dead straight from a story.
Who will the nomads deliver letters for, if the city dies for real?
Moran?
The second shipment of bodies has arrived from Na Saoirsí. I must make the preparations.
At this rate, it won't be long until the third, fourth, or more.
Are we really going to ignore it?
As I've told you many times, the wakemen do not take sides. It is not our decision to make.
Are the Na Saoirsíans not Tarans too? Why are we standing on the side watching, when the Taran capital is marching towards destruction?
I know you came back with the red dragon. You fear she will meet the same fate as Labhréathach.
But we were the ones to send Labhréathach to his doom.
You...
We are descended from the priests that once officiated the ceremonies of Tara's royal palace.
Our ancestors, moved by Labhréathach's plea, welcomed him back into the palace when Ailill's stance was ambivalent.
The flames of death that spread through the plains of Tara were only extinguished seven days after his death, when our ancestors finally returned his fire to him.
They then left the palace of their own accord, going into self-exile on Labhréathach's former pastures, so as to never forget the lesson. And thus, the wakemen were born.
...I'm sorry.
But Reed isn't Labhréathach, and history is not a cycle that repeats itself endlessly.
If it were, Reed would have already died in that citadel.
Even her tyrant of a sister was the one to free Tara from Victorian rule.
Fates are often alike, but always differ where it matters most.
'Fates are often alike, but always differ where it matters most.'
What a wonderful tagline for the cover of the book. Surely this one is destined for the best-seller charts.
Assuming, of course, that you are ever able to finish the story.
I've never intended to profit off it.
Oh, you will. If not in money, then in fame.
The basis of the story, where it happened... There are more than enough reasons this isn't my story to tell.
Besides, what happens next?
That's what I wanted to ask. Did Loughshinny go back?
From this point on, what I've seen, what I believe, and what I've said all contradict one another. Which is why I've lost sight of the ending.
Feel free to take up the challenge, if you're up to it. Give this dance between the dragon and her reflection whatever ending you please.
Oh? Is it all the same if I tell it myself?
Of course, ma'am. As you will it—
The writer raises his mug, which is almost empty now, and toasts the woman before him.
Once again, he swallows the bitter brew, half-expecting the sweet aftertaste that he knows will never come.
Did I spook you? You got so polite all of a sudden. It makes me feel impertinent for even pressing the question.
No. But I must make it abundantly clear that everything we have discussed until now is no more than idle chatter. You understand, don't you?
The woman sits straight and does not say a word. The writer can only attempt to guess whether she is still smiling through the shadows.
Well? What are you waiting for? I've nearly finished my drink.
I've fallen into the same conundrum as you.
If this is as far as both your references and your imagination go, how could I possibly put the red dragon's ultimate fate to paper?
I've already said all I'm able to. I refuse to give this story an entirely fictional end, one that not even I could believe in. Even if the alternative is that there is no ending.
If you don't have an answer either, then no need to waste your time here. Leave while it's still early.
Is this because you do not remember, do not understand, or is it because you wish you never knew, never understood?
—What?
What are you getting at?
You've never 'lost sight of the ending.' It would be more appropriate to say that your eyes have become clouded by too many 'creative liberties'. Shall I recount them for you?
Treasures leading people into the darkness.
Culann dragging the statue to the square by himself.

And virtually everything related to the dark mist in the woods: from the rejection of Brigid, to the food carts returning to the city on their own, to Loughshinny becoming lost in the woods, to The Brigadier and Eblana's meeting in the city.
If these 'creative liberties' have created the contradictions in your mind, then it is my responsibility to set them on a path towards the ending.
Shall I supplement the story with my own observations?
I'll take that as a yes.
Perhaps they will lead you to a conclusion you can be satisfied with.

lines.st: Where did you leave off...? Ah yes, on the eve of Samhain, while the sun still hung in the sky.
Samhain nights bind life with death. This is no mere superstition. Or perhaps you could say it has long transcended superstition.
Taran legends speak of countless great deeds and tragedies occurring on this very night, and such tales often put the date front and center.

So long as innumerable legends coincide on that day, and so long as the Tarans still remember, if only vaguely, to celebrate the harvest and light up fires on that day, it will never be a simple change of season.
It itself is the perfect footnote to add to any myth.
...Oh, and another thing. I don't suppose you'd mind if I gave the characters more fitting names, yes?
The sun is leaning westward.
Have we yet to figure out how to go about this final confrontation?

lines.x: Loughshinny, why are you hiding in your room at dinnertime again?
Is Dad angry?
No, but he will be soon.
I'll be down soon—
What's that on your desk?
Nothing!
The young red dragon tries to block her sister's line of sight with her body, but her effort is in vain.
A small paper box sits on her desk, carefully lined with cotton. Inside lies a tiny fowlbeast—already dead but still harboring a faint lingering warmth.
It seems to have broken its leg while alive. An unsuccessful splint fashioned from stiff paper now lies in pieces around it, accompanied by a few grains of wheat.
This is the fowlbeast that sang at my window every morning. I don't know what happened to it, but... I wanted to save it, and...
I beg of you, Sister—Tell Dad I'm only feeling ill. I'll be on time for dinner tomorrow... I'll bury it in the courtyard tonight.
So you're giving up?
It's already dead.
Is that so?
Purple flames slip past the fowlbeast's feathers into its already cold body.
The two watch as it slowly stands up, its broken leg stiffly propping itself up on the cotton.
Alright. Would you like it to fly, or to sing a song for you?
N-No! This isn't right! Not like this!
Then why didn't you use your own flames on it while it was still alive?
Your fowlbeast could have lived a much longer life. You do realize you aren't limited to how the mediocre masses do things, don't you?
But Mum told me to swallow my flames, hide them away, and never let them be seen...
And who would see them in your own room?
The young dragon looks away to avoid her sister's eyes, but finds her gaze landing on the fowlbeast standing on its cotton bed instead.
Purple fire burns in its eyes, and even the way it tilts its head is the spitting image of her sister.

lines.st: Or do we perhaps still harbor unrealistic fantasies for how this could end?

lines.x: Sister, you're covered in blood... were you injured?!
Most of it isn't mine. The Duke's spy bled profusely.
I'll replace your bandages.
Where is the Earl of Warwick?
He has... work to do.
Yes, so he says. And as always, you take him at his word.
He even allowed the spy to discuss poetry with you while he 'works'.
I thought I'd been keeping mum about our origins, as well as anything related to the Earl's vision...
Everyone in this manor knows you never say a word more than necessary. That has never been something the Earl would bother to check.
That's right. Had we let the spy escape, the Earl would still inevitably find a way to silence him in the dark, and we'd simply find ourselves bound by yet another chain called caution.
I'm sorry, I—
There's no need for you to apologize. Apologies have never changed a thing.
Once packed with guests, the long-empty parlor's acoustics now amplify the sound of the bandages being ripped apart, making the atmosphere even heavier.
Those poems by that Seamus Williams you've been so into... If you can remember them, why not recite a line or two for me?
The noble red dragon racks her brains for a verse that would not rouse her sister's rage. Perhaps something related to Tara, brave warriors, or even ancient legends—
But all she can recall are verses that stand aloof from worldly affairs.
'The land is but a strange romance, the cause of its end lost to chance ill.'
'I am but a wandering bard, well-versed in lore from songs in the woods...'
She sneaks a peek at her sister's face and sees no change, so she plucks up her courage and continues to recite more lines.
Only when she finishes both the entire poem and the new bandage with a delicate knot does she realize that her sister has already fallen asleep, encircled by meter and rhyme.

lines.st: Or perhaps...?

lines.x: Sister.
Ah, Loughshinny. You've kept me waiting.
What of your new friends? The wayward wakewoman, or perhaps that lively nomad?
With your presence, any obstacles preventing you from bringing them along should have easily been reduced to naught.
They're in the city, but this is a moment to be shared by us alone.
Indeed.
It's cold out here on the balcony, so let us continue this in the corridor.
A replica of where Labhréathach died, in Na Saoirsí... Why must you cling onto our inglorious history and repeat it?
I see you've gained a deeper understanding of the word Tara.
But if history does not repeat itself, then how can we speak of breaking the cycle?

lines.st: It can be very difficult to tell us apart by appearance.
But the moment either of us speaks, it becomes as clear as night and day.
Eblana only ever steers the conversation toward her own interests, while Loughshinny is ever the polite one.
She'd get to the bottom of this and find an ending—
Don't interrupt me, sir. I'm talking about the characters in the story.

lines.x: I'm sorry, Eblana.
No, I'm quite glad. You finally accepted my invitation.
To dance?
Yes. To dance.
I'll dance with you to the end, together. However...
Please tell me first. The last time we met, you said you couldn't afford to wait any longer... What exactly is going on?

With that same smile of hers, the older sister stands at the end of the corridor, while the younger clutches her hands together and hides in the shadow of a wall lamp, hoping the other party won't notice her heart wavering.
